close_reason: Implemented no-op detection and reindentation in fuzzy match path (commit bb1c8cc)
description:
HydraCoder (30B MoE) generates edit_file calls where old_content doesn't exactly match the file text — typically whitespace differences (extra/missing spaces, indentation). The current whitespace-normalized fallback finds a match but often applies a no-op replacement because the new_content also has the same whitespace differences.

Two approaches to fix:
1. **Smarter fuzzy matching**: strip leading/trailing whitespace per line in both old_content and file text, then match. If found, apply new_content preserving the file's original indentation.
2. **Line-number-based edit mode**: add optional start_line/end_line params to edit_file so the model can specify a line range instead of exact text matching. This is more robust when the model has seen truncated file content.

Evidence from dogfooding: 3/6 iterations had edit_file calls that 'succeeded' via whitespace-normalized match but produced no actual file changes. The model understands what to change but can't produce exact text matches from truncated content.

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/tools/patch_tool.rs
title: Improve edit_file fuzzy matching for local model whitespace errors

description:
Bridge the coordination crate's MCP server tools into Rig agent context so agents can invoke coordination capabilities (verifier, escalation, ensemble, harness) natively.

Background: The coordination crate runs as an MCP server via rmcp exposing tools over stdio. Rig has native MCP client support. Currently these are two disconnected systems — Rig agents can't call coordination tools directly.

Pattern from rig-examples:
```rust
let mcp_client = ClientBuilder::new(transport).build();
mcp_client.open().await?;
let tools = mcp_client.list_tools(None, None).await?;

let agent = tools.tools.into_iter()
    .fold(agent_builder, |builder, tool| {
        builder.mcp_tool(tool, mcp_client.clone().into())
    })
    .build();
```

Implementation:
1. Start coordination MCP server as a child process (or in-process via stdio pipe)
2. Create Rig MCP client connecting to coordination's stdio transport
3. Discover and fold coordination tools into manager agent builder
4. Map coordination tool names to Rig tool interface
5. Handle error propagation from MCP tool calls back to agent context
6. Test round-trip: Rig agent calls verify_code → coordination runs verifier → result back to agent

Key coordination tools to bridge:
- verify_code (quality gate pipeline)
- escalate_task (tier routing)
- query_ensemble (multi-model voting)
- create_checkpoint / rollback_checkpoint (harness)
- submit_work_packet (context handoff)

Acceptance: Manager agent can invoke coordination MCP tools natively through Rig's tool interface with proper error handling.
title: MCP bridge: fold coordination tools into Rig agents

description:
Replace per-agent tool construction with a shared ToolServer handle for vasp-02's 4-slot Implementer.

Background: Rig v0.22+ provides ToolServer which creates a Clone-able ToolServerHandle. Multiple concurrent agent sessions can share the same tool set without Arc/RwLock overhead.

Pattern from rig-examples:
```rust
let tool_server: ToolServerHandle = ToolServer::new()
    .tool(ReadFileTool::new(working_dir.clone()))
    .tool(WriteFileTool::new(working_dir.clone()))
    .tool(EditFileTool::new(working_dir.clone()))
    .tool(RunCommandTool::new(working_dir.clone()))
    .run();

// Clone handle for each concurrent agent session
let agent = client.agent("Qwen3.5-397B-A17B")
    .tool_server(tool_server.clone())
    .build();
```

Implementation:
1. Add ToolServer construction in orchestrator initialization
2. Replace per-agent .tool() chains with .tool_server(handle.clone())
3. Verify sandbox enforcement still works through ToolServer dispatch
4. Load test with 4 concurrent sessions on vasp-02
5. Measure memory/lock contention improvement vs current approach

Acceptance: 4 concurrent Implementer sessions share tools via ToolServer with no regressions in sandbox enforcement or tool correctness.
title: ToolServer for concurrent Implementer sessions

description: Run cargo check --message-format=json after every edit_file call and feed diagnostics back into the LLM context immediately, not just at full verifier time. OpenCode's LSP integration catches type mismatches, undefined variables, import errors after each edit (~150ms). For us this would be ~10-30s per edit (cargo check on affected crate). Trade-off: slower per-edit but prevents the agent from going off the rails with cascading errors. Could run on just the affected crate to reduce time.
title: Post-edit cargo check diagnostics (OpenCode LSP pattern)

close_reason: Not suitable: Rig Extractor is designed for data EXTRACTION (text->struct), not GENERATION (task->plan). Hardcoded preamble ('extract structured data from text') conflicts with our custom agent preambles and cannot be replaced (append_preamble only). Tool-equipped agents (planner, fixer, breaker) cannot use Extractor since it only supports its submit tool. Reviewer's nuanced fail-closed fallback (schema_valid, custom blocking_issues) is more robust than Extractor's simple retry. Current manual JSON parsing (extract_json_block + serde_json::from_str) is the right pattern for generation-style LLM calls.
description: Replace manual extract_json_block() + serde_json::from_str() with Rig's client.extractor::<T>(model) pattern. Add #[derive(JsonSchema)] to SubtaskPlan, ReviewResult, PlannerOutput structs. Eliminates ~150 lines of manual JSON parsing. Rig's Extractor uses schemars to auto-generate JSON schema and returns typed T directly. Files: contracts.rs, subtask.rs, reviewer.rs, specialists.rs. Requires: schemars = '0.8' (already in Cargo.toml)
title: Adopt Rig Extractor for structured output (reviewer, planner, subtask)

close_reason: VerificationTracker with AcceptanceStatus lifecycle (Candidate→Probation→Accepted/Rejected). AcceptancePolicy with configurable thresholds. 15 new tests, 668 coordination total passing.
description:
Phase 4 Safety. The skill library and calibrated thresholds are forms of self-modification — the swarm changes its own behavior based on experience. Implement conservative acceptance: new skills/thresholds must pass a validation period before being promoted to active use. Prevents a single lucky session from permanently biasing routing or escalation.

Depends on: issues 10 (skill library), 12 (escalation heuristics)
Key files: NEW coordination/src/analytics/verification.rs, coordination/src/analytics/skills.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs
Crates: coordination + swarm-agents

Acceptance criteria:
- AcceptanceStatus enum: Candidate, Probation(uses_remaining), Accepted, Rejected
- AcceptancePolicy: min_uses_before_promotion (default: 5), min_success_rate (default: 0.7), probation_period (default: 10 uses)
- VerificationTracker: track_usage(skill_id, outcome) -> AcceptanceStatus transition
- Skills start as Candidate, move to Probation after first success, promoted to Accepted after meeting policy thresholds
- Rejected skills are soft-deleted (kept for analysis but not matched)
- Escalation thresholds follow same acceptance pipeline
- Orchestrator only uses Accepted skills/thresholds by default, Probation ones marked as experimental
- Unit tests: full lifecycle Candidate → Probation → Accepted, Candidate → Probation → Rejected
title: Implement conservative acceptance for self-modifications

description: CLIAPIProxy v6.8+ uses x-api-key header for static API key auth. The proxy config has oauth-model-alias entries under the antigravity provider (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-5 → gemini-claude-sonnet-4-5), but these aliases only apply to OAuth-authenticated sessions, not API key auth. This means requests via x-api-key for claude-opus-4-6-thinking or claude-sonnet-4-5 fail with 502 'unknown provider for model'. Either: (1) add model aliases for API key auth in proxy config, or (2) update swarm to send pre-aliased model names when using API key auth. Config: /opt/cli-proxy-api/config.yaml on ai-proxy.
title: CLIAPIProxy API key auth does not use OAuth model aliases

description:
Phase 3 Skill Library. Validate that packed context (SessionSummary in WorkPacket) actually preserves task-relevant information. Use probe questions: generate N factual questions from the full session, then check if answers are recoverable from the compressed summary alone. If probe pass rate drops below threshold, the summary is too lossy.

Depends on: issue 3 (structured summaries)
Key files: NEW coordination/src/context_packer/probes.rs, coordination/src/context_packer/packer.rs
Crate: coordination

Acceptance criteria:
- Probe struct: question, expected_answer, answer_source (which iteration/field)
- ProbeGenerator: generate_probes(full_session: &[IterationDelta]) -> Vec<Probe> (deterministic, template-based)
- ProbeEvaluator: evaluate(summary: &SessionSummary, probes: &[Probe]) -> ProbeResults
- ProbeResults: pass_count, fail_count, pass_rate, failed_probes (for debugging)
- Threshold configuration: minimum pass rate (default 0.8)
- Integration: ContextPacker runs probes after packing, warns if below threshold
- Unit tests: probe generation, evaluation with good/bad summaries
title: Implement probe-based context quality evaluation

description: Replace grep-only file targeting with a ranked candidate pipeline using explicit paths, symbol search, verifier file paths, package boundaries, related tests, and recent git changes. Split context into writable_files vs read_only_context. Based on patterns from Aider (repo map with PageRank), OpenCode (ranked retrieval), and Codex (file targeting). Goal: reduce wasted initial reads and improve target precision@5.
title: Epic F: Smart File Targeting & Context Pipeline

close_reason: Fixed: orchestrator.rs was creating a separate ClusterHealth::from_config() instance for the preflight check. Added EndpointPool::cluster_health() and AgentFactory::cluster_health() accessors, then wired the orchestrator to use factory.cluster_health().cloned().unwrap_or_else(|| ClusterHealth::from_config(config)) so both share the same Arc<RwLock> status map. Commit 969fa97.
description: In endpoint_pool.rs, the health-aware routing logs 'All endpoints report down — using default round-robin' on every call even when ClusterHealth shows all 3 endpoints as healthy. Root cause unknown — likely: (1) TIER_NAMES constant doesn't match keys written by check_all_endpoints(), or (2) is_usable() returns false for a status that check_all_endpoints() considers healthy, or (3) try_read_status() returns None (write lock held) so falls through to 'all down'. Observed in dogfood-vbjk-20260307-2131.log at 21:32:13.885296Z, right after preflight shows healthy=3/3. Functionally harmless (falls back to round-robin = correct default) but health-aware routing never actually activates.
title: endpoint_pool: health-aware routing always falls back (status map keys mismatch)

description:

Scope:
- Apply all recommended Rig adoption patterns identified in the deep examples review.
- Add an intelligent model router that chooses local/cloud workers by task complexity, historical success, latency, and budget.
- Introduce deterministic orchestration state transitions, stronger contracts, and rollout controls.

2) Higher first-pass verifier success.
3) Better incident debugability via typed traces and OTel spans.
4) Lower routing cost through adaptive policy selection.

description:
Phase 2 Signal Detection. Detect behavioral friction — sessions where the agent is stuck but error counts alone don't reveal it. Friction signals: file churn (modifying same file 3+ times), error cycling (same error category recurring after supposed fix), stagnant error count (errors not decreasing across iterations), context overflow (work packet exceeds size threshold).

Depends on: issue 4 (artifact tracking for file churn data)
Key files: NEW coordination/src/analytics/friction.rs, coordination/src/work_packet/types.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs
Crates: coordination + swarm-agents

Acceptance criteria:
- FrictionSignal enum: FileChurn(path, count), ErrorCycling(category, occurrences), StagnantErrors(count, iterations), ContextOverflow(size_bytes)
- FrictionDetector with detect(session: &SessionTelemetry) -> Vec<FrictionSignal>
- Configurable thresholds per signal type
- Integration: orchestrator checks friction mid-session (not just at end) to enable early escalation
- Unit tests: synthetic sessions triggering each friction type
title: Implement friction detection signals

description: Formally separate reasoning from editing. Cloud manager emits natural language proposals (not WorkPackets with file contexts). Local worker receives only: (a) the proposal, (b) target files, (c) current file content. No error history, no escalation context. Evidence: o1-preview (Architect) + DeepSeek (Editor) = 85% on SWE-bench. Our system is already close to this pattern but mixes reasoning and editing concerns in the WorkPacket. Benefit: Qwen3.5 only needs to be good at applying changes, not reasoning about architecture.
title: Formal architect/editor separation (Aider pattern)

description:
Use Rig's parallel\! pipeline macro to formalize concurrent Architect + Implementer analysis, replacing ad-hoc spawn-and-join patterns.

Background: The swarm currently runs Architect and Implementer analysis sequentially or with manual tokio::spawn. Rig's pipeline system provides typed, composable parallel execution with automatic result aggregation.

Pattern from rig-examples:
```rust
let pipeline = pipeline::new()
    .chain(parallel\!(
        passthrough(),                    // Original task context
        extract(architect_agent),          // Architecture analysis
        extract(implementer_agent),        // Implementation plan
    ))
    .map(|(context, arch_review, impl_plan)| {
        // Merge architect review + implementation plan
        WorkPacket::new(context, arch_review, impl_plan)
    });
```

Implementation:
1. Define typed pipeline stages for the swarm's core flows:
   a. Pre-task: parallel(classify_task, check_notebook, analyze_dependencies)
   b. Planning: parallel(architect_review, implementer_plan)
   c. Post-verify: parallel(update_notebook, log_telemetry, update_beads)
2. Replace sequential analysis calls with pipeline composition
3. Add error handling for partial pipeline failures (one branch fails, others succeed)
4. Benchmark throughput improvement vs sequential execution

Acceptance: Core analysis flows use typed Rig pipelines with parallel execution and measurable latency reduction.
title: Parallel pipeline with parallel\! macro for concurrent analysis

description:
read_file currently returns the entire file (or truncated first N chars via SWARM_READ_FILE_MAX_CHARS). When a file is larger than the truncation limit (default 6000 chars), the model can only see the top portion and can't edit content later in the file.

Add optional start_line/end_line parameters to read_file so the model can request specific sections. This enables a workflow where the model:
1. Reads the file header/structure (default read)
2. Reads specific functions or sections by line range
3. Edits those sections with accurate old_content

This is especially important for local models with small context windows where full file content would suppress tool calls.

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/tools/fs_tools.rs
title: Add line-range read_file variant for targeted file reading

close_reason: Fixed in 14afadf: 4 bugs in file targeting pipeline
description: Four interrelated bugs prevent the compact prompt from targeting the right files: (1) BeadsIssue struct lacks description field — bd show --json returns it but we discard it. (2) CLI --issue mode doesn't try beads lookup — defaults to 'CLI issue: {id}' objective with zero useful content. (3) Objective never includes description text — even batch issues only use title. (4) File path extraction requires / separator — bare filenames like runtime_adapter.rs are rejected. Fix: add description to BeadsIssue, try bd show for CLI issues, include description in objective text, accept .rs/.toml suffixes without directory prefix.
title: Fix file targeting pipeline: use beads description + accept bare filenames

Acceptance: cargo build -p swarm-agents compiles. Non-http URLs produce an immediate error return.

description: All 3 Qwen3.5-397B-A17B llama-server instances (vasp-01/02/03, build b8179) crash when the swarm sends POST /v1/chat/completions with tool/function definitions. GET /health works fine — the crash is specifically in the inference path with tools. The server segfaults immediately on the first tool-bearing completion request. This was discovered during nightly-01 dogfood retry when all servers went down sequentially. Possible causes: (1) Jinja template bug with Qwen3.5's chat template and tool definitions, (2) tool schema exceeds buffer limits, (3) KV cache OOM from expanded context with tools. Blocked: all dogfood runs until resolved. Next steps: check llama.cpp issues for Qwen3.5 tool calling bugs, try with --jinja disabled, try with fewer tools registered.
notes: CONFIRMED: Server crashes specifically when tools/functions are included in the chat completions request. Simple completions without tools work fine. The crash happens in the Jinja template processing of tool definitions. Test: simple curl with just messages=OK, curl with tools=[{...}]=CRASH. This means beefcake-qau8 (tool_calls=0) is NOT a model issue - the server dies before the model can generate any response.
title: llama-server crashes on /v1/chat/completions with tool definitions

description: git write-tree before each worker iteration, git read-tree on failure. Prevents compounding errors across iterations where a bad edit in iteration 1 causes cascading failures in iterations 2-10. Implementation: In orchestrator loop, capture git tree hash before worker runs. On verifier failure, restore tree state and retry with error context. OpenCode uses this successfully for immediate rollback.
title: Snapshot-based rollback per iteration (OpenCode pattern)

description: The work packet should explicitly mark which files the worker may edit vs which are context-only. Enforce this in EditFileTool — reject edits to files not in the writable set. This prevents workers from editing shared dependencies or unrelated files. Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/tools/fs_tools.rs.
title: Distinguish writable_files from read_only_context in work packets

close_reason: Resolved by swarm orchestrator: NLM query failsafe with query_kb_with_failsafe() wrapper, 3 call sites updated, 2 tests (1 iteration, 9m35s)
description: The orchestrator queries NotebookLM (project_brain, debugging_kb) before each iteration for context enrichment. When nlm query fails (e.g., auth expired, network timeout, nlm binary missing), it should degrade gracefully rather than propagating the error. Current behavior: the error is caught but the enrichment silently fails with an empty context addition. Need: explicit warning log, retry with exponential backoff (1 attempt), and a circuit breaker that disables NLM queries for the rest of the session after N consecutive failures. Also add a SWARM_NLM_ENABLED=0 env var to disable NLM queries entirely for testing.
notes: Swarm run 2026-02-21: partial implementation — added graceful degradation in notebook_tool.rs call() + 2 tests (AlwaysFailKnowledgeBase mock, empty response handling). Missing: NlmCircuitBreaker struct, SWARM_NLM_ENABLED env var, config fields, retry with backoff. proxy_write_file/proxy_edit_file issue is now fixed (commit 17067c2).
title: Add failsafe for NotebookLM query failures in orchestrator

Acceptance: cargo build -p swarm-agents compiles. cargo test -p swarm-agents passes. After 5 failures, probing is skipped for 60s (verify with a unit test).

Constraints: Do NOT modify any other file. Add the circuit breaker state to the existing endpoint tracking struct.

description: Safari on iOS requires HTTPS for any feature beyond basic HTTP (PWA install, some fetch APIs). The current maitai daemon serves HTTP on port 50051 (gRPC) and 8080 (WASM). For grpc-web in production on iPad, the WASM app origin and the gRPC-web proxy must either share the same origin (same port with a reverse proxy) or use HTTPS with a valid cert. Options: (1) mkcert for local LAN cert (simplest for lab use), (2) nginx/Caddy reverse proxy on maitai terminating TLS and proxying both WASM files and grpc-web. Document the chosen approach in scripts/deploy-maitai.sh. Note: if grpc-web works over HTTP on the lab LAN (T0.1 validation may reveal this), this task may be deferred.
title: T2.3: HTTPS/TLS deployment for grpc-web from maitai

description: Replace alphabetical file walking in the context packer with graph-based ranking. Aider's repo map: tree-sitter parses all .rs files for symbol defs, builds dependency graph (NetworkX MultiDiGraph), runs PageRank with personalization biased toward task-mentioned files, binary-searches to fit token budget. This directly solves our problem: 402 .rs files, only 18 fit in 7471-token budget, all from coordination/src/ (alphabetically first), never reaching crates/swarm-agents/src/. Simpler alternative: use cargo metadata + use-statement analysis instead of full tree-sitter. Use tree-sitter Rust crate.
title: Repo map via tree-sitter (Aider PageRank pattern)

description:
Apply Codex-recommended debugging steps to fix the Qwen3.5-397B chat completions issue where the model produces flat/random logits after chat template special tokens.

Symptom: /v1/completions works perfectly (sharp logits, coherent output). /v1/chat/completions produces garbage or immediate EOS after processing <|im_start|>, <|im_end|>, <think> tokens. CPU-only reproduces it, ruling out GPU/offload issues.

Codex analysis (2026-02-25): Points to chat-path/template-token/tokenizer-metadata interaction, NOT GPU offloading or quantization.

Debugging steps (priority order):
1. A/B test: render prompt with /apply-template, feed exact string to /v1/completions
   - If works: bug is in chat completions server path (message assembly/prefill/reasoning)
   - If fails: bug is in template tokens / tokenizer metadata / GGUF
2. Try all chat server flags together: --reasoning-format none --reasoning-budget 0 --no-prefill-assistant --no-cache-prompt --cache-reuse 0
3. Compare /tokenize output for <|im_start|> (248045), <|im_end|> (248046), <think> against upstream HF tokenizer for Qwen3.5-MoE — check for GGUF tokenizer metadata mismatch
4. Upgrade llama.cpp past b8145:
   - PR #19849 (merged 2026-02-25): hybrid/recurrent server checkpointing fix (n_past vs position)
   - PR #19866 (merged 2026-02-24): Qwen 3.5 multi-GPU graph split ordering
   - PR #19877 (merged 2026-02-25): re-enable multimodal prompt caching
5. Try a different GGUF source for Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (different conversion pipeline)
6. If all else fails: plain-text instruct scaffold avoiding special tokens entirely (System:/User:/Assistant: format via completions endpoint)

Current state:
- vasp-01: Job 1807 running with OLD config (no --jinja, proxy on :8181)
- vasp-02: No SLURM job running
- Chat proxy deployed at /cluster/shared/scripts/llama-cpp/chat-proxy/proxy.py
- Best result so far: 57 tokens with --chat-template-kwargs '{"enable_thinking": false}' (garbled quality)
- Tracked upstream: llama.cpp #19690, #19858

Acceptance: Qwen3.5-397B responds coherently to chat completions requests with >200 token outputs and sharp logits, OR a reliable workaround is documented and integrated into the proxy.
notes: Q4_K_M download COMPLETE on vasp-02 (all 7 shards, ~241GB). Downloaded via hf-mirror.com at ~50MB/s through pve1 pipeline. UD-Q4_K_XL confirmed broken. Next: start Q4_K_M on vasp-02 with b8179 binary and test chat completions. This is debugging step 5 (different GGUF source). Start script: /tmp/start-qwen35-q4km.sh
title: Unblock Qwen3.5-397B chat completions for dogfooding

description: When tool_choice=required forces multiple edit_file calls in one agent iteration, the model references its own previous edit text as old_content for subsequent edits. After the first fuzzy-matched edit rewrites the file (with reindentation), the model's cached version of the code no longer matches the actual file content. Both exact and fuzzy match fail on turns 4-5. Discovered in dogfood run 4. Possible fixes: (1) Include edit_file success feedback that shows the actual written content, (2) After a successful edit, force a read_file on the next turn, (3) Return the relevant portion of the new file content in the edit_file success response so the model has current state.

description:

Fix: Extract a SourceFileProvider struct that:

This eliminates redundant disk reads and provides a single point for file access patterns.

description: Research SurrealDB deployment best practices via PAL consensus. Compare embedded (RocksDB backend) vs networked (TiKV), auth/TLS setup, systemd config, resource requirements for ai-proxy LXC. Include SurrealDB 2.x architecture (compute/storage separation). Output: deployment decision document.
notes:
PAL consensus prompt: Compare SurrealDB embedded (RocksDB) vs networked (TiKV) for a single-node LXC deployment. Consider: memory footprint, persistence guarantees, auth setup, systemd integration. Expected output: config template + systemd unit.

## RESEARCH OUTPUT: SurrealDB Deployment for HPC/LXC

### Executive Decision
**RECOMMENDATION: Embedded RocksDB mode on ai-proxy LXC**

TiKV is unnecessary for a single-node deployment. RocksDB embedded mode provides the best performance with zero network overhead, simpler operations, and lower resource consumption. TiKV only makes sense for multi-node HA deployments.

---

### 1. Architecture Overview

SurrealDB separates compute (query layer) and storage layers:
- **Query Layer**: Parser → Executor → Iterator → Document Processor (stateless)
- **Storage Layer**: RocksDB (default), SurrealKV (experimental), TiKV (distributed)

For single-node: compute + storage run in the same process. No external dependencies.

### 2. Storage Engine Comparison (Single-Node Context)

| Aspect | RocksDB (Embedded) | TiKV (Single-Node) | SurrealKV |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------| 
| Maturity | Industry standard (Meta) | Production-ready but overkill | Experimental, Rust-native |
| Dependencies | C++ lib (compiles with SurrealDB) | Separate PD + TiKV services | None (pure Rust) |
| Performance | ~508k reads/s, ~155k writes/s | Same (TiKV uses RocksDB internally) + network overhead | Not yet benchmarked |
| Operational complexity | Minimal — single binary | High — 3 separate daemons | Minimal |
| HA/Replication | None (single-node) | Multi-raft (overkill for 1 node) | None |
| Features | Standard KV | Distributed transactions | Time-travel queries, versioned data |

**Decision: RocksDB.** TiKV adds PD + TiKV daemon overhead with zero benefit on single node. SurrealKV is still experimental.

### 3. Resource Requirements

**Minimum (ai-proxy LXC):**
- CPU: 1+ vCPU (SurrealDB Cloud free tier runs on 0.25 vCPU)
- RAM: 512 MB minimum, 2-4 GB recommended for knowledge graph + vector indexes
- Disk: SSD/NVMe preferred (RocksDB LSM-tree optimized for SSDs)
- The HNSW vector index uses a bounded memory cache (default 256 MiB, configurable)

**Production recommended (for reference):**
- 4+ cores, 8+ GB RAM, NVMe/SSD, no swap

For our UKG use case on ai-proxy, 2-4 GB RAM should be sufficient. RocksDB dynamically calculates block cache size based on available memory (`SURREAL_ROCKSDB_BLOCK_CACHE_SIZE`).

### 4. Installation

```bash
# Install SurrealDB binary
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://install.surrealdb.com | sh

# Verify
surreal version
```

### 5. Start Command (Production Config)

```bash
surreal start \
  --log info \
  --user root \
  --pass '<STRONG_PASSWORD>' \
  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 \
  rocksdb:///var/lib/surrealdb/data
```

Key flags:
- `--bind 0.0.0.0:8000` — Listen on all interfaces (required for 10.0.0.0/24 access from compute nodes)
- `--log info` — Production log level (debug/trace impact performance)
- `--query-timeout <duration>` — Optional max query execution time
- `--transaction-timeout <duration>` — Optional max transaction time
- `--web-crt` / `--web-key` — TLS certificate/key paths (optional for internal network)
- `--allow-net` / `--deny-net` — Control outbound network capabilities
- `--allow-funcs` / `--deny-funcs` — Restrict available SurrealQL functions

### 6. Systemd Unit File

```ini
[Unit]
Description=SurrealDB Server
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=surrealdb
Group=surrealdb
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/surreal start \
  --log info \
  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 \
  rocksdb:///var/lib/surrealdb/data
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
LimitNOFILE=65536
Environment=SURREAL_ROCKSDB_BLOCK_CACHE_SIZE=536870912
Environment=SURREAL_SYNC_DATA=true

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Notes:
- Root user credentials persist after first start (no need in subsequent starts)
- `LimitNOFILE=65536` — RocksDB opens many file descriptors
- `SURREAL_SYNC_DATA=true` — Ensures write durability (default)
- `SURREAL_ROCKSDB_BLOCK_CACHE_SIZE=536870912` — 512 MB block cache (tune based on available RAM)

### 7. Environment Variables (Key Tuning)

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `SURREAL_BIND` | 127.0.0.1:8000 | Bind address |
| `SURREAL_LOG` | info | Log level |
| `SURREAL_ROCKSDB_BLOCK_CACHE_SIZE` | Auto (based on RAM) | Read cache size |
| `SURREAL_ROCKSDB_WRITE_BUFFER_SIZE` | Default | Write buffer memory |
| `SURREAL_ROCKSDB_MAX_WRITE_BUFFER_NUMBER` | Default | Max concurrent write buffers |
| `SURREAL_SYNC_DATA` | true | Fsync writes to disk |
| `SURREAL_QUERY_TIMEOUT` | None | Max query execution time |

### 8. Authentication & Security

- **Auth enabled by default** since SurrealDB 2.0 (good)
- First start with `--user`/`--pass` creates OWNER-role root user, persisted in storage
- For internal HPC network: TLS optional but recommended if traversing untrusted segments
- Restrict capabilities with `--deny-net`, `--deny-funcs` to minimize attack surface

### 9. Network Access from Compute Nodes

ai-proxy LXC (100.105.113.58) is accessible from compute nodes on 10.0.0.0/24.
- Bind to `0.0.0.0:8000` (or specific interface)
- Compute nodes connect via: `http://100.105.113.58:8000` or the 10.x.x.x address
- SurrealDB exposes HTTP/WebSocket APIs on the bind port
- Rust SDK connects via: `Surreal::new::<Ws>("100.105.113.58:8000")`

### 10. Graph + Vector Capabilities (UKG-Relevant)

**Graph:**
- `RELATE` statement creates typed edges between records (vertex → edge → vertex)
- Graph traversal via `->` syntax: `SELECT * FROM crate:tokio->depends_on->crate`
- Edges are first-class records with metadata fields
- No JOINs needed — record links provide direct traversal

**Vector Search:**
- MTREE index: `DEFINE INDEX idx ON table FIELDS embedding MTREE DIMENSION 768 DIST COSINE TYPE F32`
- HNSW index: Faster ANN with tunable M and EFC parameters, 256 MiB default cache
- kNN queries: `WHERE embedding <|K|> $query_vector`
- Similarity functions: `vector::similarity::cosine()`, `vector::similarity::euclidean()`, etc.

**Combined Graph+Vector (GraphRAG):**
- Can traverse graph edges AND do vector similarity in the same query
- Ideal for knowledge graph enrichment: find related code entities via graph, rank by vector similarity

### 11. Deployment Steps

```bash
# 1. On ai-proxy LXC:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://install.surrealdb.com | sh

# 2. Create service user + data dir
useradd -r -s /bin/false surrealdb
mkdir -p /var/lib/surrealdb/data
chown surrealdb:surrealdb /var/lib/surrealdb/data

# 3. Install systemd unit (see above)
# 4. Enable and start
systemctl enable surrealdb
systemctl start surrealdb

# 5. Initialize root credentials
surreal start --user root --pass '<PASSWORD>' --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 rocksdb:///var/lib/surrealdb/data
# (first run only, then ctrl-c and let systemd manage)

# 6. Verify from compute node
curl http://100.105.113.58:8000/health
```

### Sources
- SurrealDB Architecture: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/introduction/architecture
- Storage & Deployment: https://surrealdb.com/learn/fundamentals/performance/deployment-storage
- CLI Start Command: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/start
- Environment Variables: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/env
- Performance Best Practices: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/reference-guide/performance-best-practices
- Vector Search: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/models/vector

close_reason: Research complete. Recommendation: Hybrid architecture — keep direct RocksDB for hot-path ensemble state, use networked SurrealDB on ai-proxy for Error KB and Archival Memory. Full comparison, schema design, and migration path documented in notes.
description: Investigate whether SurrealDB (which uses RocksDB as its default storage backend) should replace the direct RocksDB approach proposed in beefcake-swarm-3r9 (Error Pattern KB) and beefcake-swarm-b30 (Archival Memory). Compare: query flexibility (SurrealQL graph+vector vs manual KV), latency overhead (SurrealDB query layer vs direct RocksDB), operational simplicity (one DB vs two). Reference SurrealDB architecture.
notes:
PAL consensus. Key insight: SurrealDB uses RocksDB under the hood — so the question is whether the query layer overhead is worth the graph/vector capabilities. If yes, 3r9 and b30 migrate to SurrealDB. If no, keep direct RocksDB for latency-sensitive operations (per-issue state) and use SurrealDB for cross-issue knowledge only.

## RESEARCH OUTPUT: SurrealDB vs Direct RocksDB for Error KB & Archival Memory

### Executive Decision
**RECOMMENDATION: Hybrid Architecture — SurrealDB for Knowledge Graph, keep direct RocksDB for hot-path state**

The key insight is that SurrealDB *uses* RocksDB under the hood. The question is whether the SurrealQL query layer overhead is justified by the graph+vector capabilities. The answer is **yes for cross-issue knowledge, no for per-issue hot-path state**.

---

### 1. Current State Analysis

**Existing Direct RocksDB (coordination/src/state/store.rs):**
- 6 column families: sessions, tasks, results, voting, context, events
- Simple KV access pattern: `put(cf, key, bincode_value)` / `get(cf, key)` / `list_keys(cf, prefix)`
- Bincode serialization for speed
- ~400 LOC, clean abstraction
- Sub-millisecond operations (direct RocksDB = no query parsing, no optimization, no transaction overhead)

**Proposed Error KB (beefcake-swarm-3r9) needs:**
- Store: `(error_signature, code_snippet, fix_diff, strategy, model_tier, iterations)`
- Query: Find similar past errors by ErrorCategory + key tokens + file structure
- Aggregate: Per-model success rates by error category
- Feed top-3 matching strategies into WorkPacket.relevant_playbooks

**Proposed Archival Memory (beefcake-swarm-b30) needs:**
- Store: Prior diffs, verifier reports, repeated errors, decisions, design notes per bead_id
- Query: Semantic recall of relevant prior approaches that failed
- Promote: Successful fix patterns → higher recall priority
- Summarize: Compress old memories to save context window tokens

---

### 2. Latency Analysis

| Operation | Direct RocksDB | SurrealDB (Embedded) | SurrealDB (Networked) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Point read | ~1-10 μs | ~50-500 μs (parse+execute+KV) | ~1-5 ms (+ network) |
| Point write | ~1-10 μs | ~100-1000 μs | ~2-10 ms |
| Prefix scan | ~10-100 μs | ~200-2000 μs | ~5-20 ms |
| Graph traversal | N/A (manual) | ~500 μs-5 ms | ~5-50 ms |
| Vector similarity | N/A (manual) | ~1-50 ms (HNSW) | ~5-100 ms |
| Complex join | N/A (manual) | ~1-10 ms | ~10-100 ms |

**Key finding:** SurrealDB query layer adds ~10-100x overhead for simple KV operations due to SurrealQL parsing, query planning, and transaction management. For the hot-path (ensemble coordination during compilation loops), this matters. For knowledge retrieval (once per issue start or escalation), it doesn't.

**Note:** One GitHub issue (#4767) reported embedded SurrealDB taking 17s vs 5s standalone for 69K record queries, suggesting the embedded Rust SDK may have performance anomalies. Worth benchmarking in our specific environment.

---

### 3. Feature Comparison for Error KB

| Requirement | Direct RocksDB | SurrealDB |
|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Store error patterns | ✅ Column family + bincode | ✅ `CREATE error_pattern SET ...` |
| Exact match (ErrorCategory) | ✅ Prefix scan on compound key | ✅ `WHERE category = 'BorrowChecker'` |
| Fuzzy match (key tokens) | ❌ Must implement manually | ✅ Full-text search + vector similarity |
| Graph: "errors → fixes → crates → files" | ❌ Manual adjacency lists in KV | ✅ `RELATE error:e1->fixed_by->fix:f1` + traversal |
| Aggregation (success rates) | ❌ Manual iteration + counting | ✅ `SELECT model_tier, math::mean(success) ... GROUP BY model_tier, category` |
| Top-K similar patterns | ❌ Must build custom index | ✅ `WHERE embedding <|3|> $query_vec` |
| Temporal queries | ⚠️ Manual timestamp keys | ✅ SurrealKV time-travel (experimental) |

**Direct RocksDB wins on:** simplicity for simple KV, zero overhead, existing code reuse.
**SurrealDB wins on:** every cross-issue query the Error KB needs.

---

### 4. Feature Comparison for Archival Memory

| Requirement | Direct RocksDB | SurrealDB |
|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Store per-bead memories | ✅ CF + `mem:{bead_id}:{type}:{ts}` | ✅ `CREATE memory SET bead_id = ...` |
| Semantic recall | ❌ Must build vector index | ✅ MTREE/HNSW + cosine similarity |
| Failed approach index | ⚠️ Manual compound keys | ✅ `SELECT * FROM memory WHERE bead_id = $id AND outcome = 'failed'` |
| Memory promotion | ⚠️ Manual update + re-index | ✅ `UPDATE memory SET priority += 1 WHERE ...` |
| Summarization storage | ✅ Simple KV | ✅ `UPDATE memory SET summary = ...` |
| Cross-bead pattern matching | ❌ Full scan required | ✅ Graph traversal + vector search |
| Letta-style tiered memory | ⚠️ Build from scratch | ✅ Natural fit: working=WorkPacket, archival=SurrealDB |

---

### 5. Operational Comparison

| Aspect | Direct RocksDB (keep existing) | SurrealDB (add new) |
|--------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| Dependencies | Already in Cargo.toml | +surrealdb crate (~slow compile due to C++ RocksDB dep) |
| Data location | Local file (same process) | Network (ai-proxy LXC) OR embedded |
| Backup | RocksDB checkpoint | SurrealDB export + RocksDB backup |
| Monitoring | Manual | SurrealDB health endpoint |
| Schema evolution | Manual migration code | SurrealQL `DEFINE TABLE/FIELD` |
| Multi-agent access | ⚠️ Single process only (RwLock) | ✅ Network API, concurrent connections |
| Failure isolation | Crash takes down coordination | Separate process, can restart independently |

**Critical finding on multi-agent access:** The current RocksDB store uses `RwLock<DB>` — only one process can open it. When the swarm scales to multiple agents on different nodes, they can't share a direct RocksDB instance. SurrealDB's network API solves this inherently.

---

### 6. Architecture Recommendation: Hybrid

```
┌─────────────────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────┐
│  coordination process       │     │  ai-proxy LXC            │
│                             │     │                          │
│  ┌─────────────────┐        │     │  ┌────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Direct RocksDB  │        │     │  │ SurrealDB          │  │
│  │ (hot-path state)│        │     │  │ (RocksDB backend)  │  │
│  │                 │        │     │  │                    │  │
│  │ • sessions      │        │     │  │ • error_pattern KB │  │
│  │ • tasks         │◄───────┼─────┼──│ • archival_memory  │  │
│  │ • results       │ query  │     │  │ • fix_strategies   │  │
│  │ • voting        │ on     │     │  │ • model_stats      │  │
│  │ • context       │ issue  │     │  │ • crate_graph      │  │
│  │ • events        │ start  │     │  │ • code_embeddings  │  │
│  └─────────────────┘        │     │  └────────────────────┘  │
│                             │     │    :8000 HTTP/WS API     │
└─────────────────────────────┘     └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Keep in direct RocksDB (hot path, per-issue):**
- Ensemble sessions, tasks, results, voting, context, events
- These are written/read hundreds of times per compilation cycle
- Microsecond latency matters here
- Single-process access is fine (one coordination MCP server)

**Move to SurrealDB (knowledge path, cross-issue):**
- Error Pattern KB (beefcake-swarm-3r9) → `error_pattern` table + graph edges
- Archival Memory (beefcake-swarm-b30) → `memory` table + vector index
- Model performance stats → `model_stats` table with aggregations
- Code structure graph (future UKG) → full graph model

**Why not embedded SurrealDB?**
1. Multi-agent access: future swarm agents on different nodes need shared KB
2. Failure isolation: KB crash shouldn't kill coordination loop
3. ai-proxy LXC has the resources and is already the gateway node
4. Operational clarity: one SurrealDB instance serves the whole cluster

---

### 7. SurrealDB Schema Design for Error KB

```sql
-- Error Pattern KB
DEFINE TABLE error_pattern SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD category ON error_pattern TYPE string;  -- ErrorCategory enum
DEFINE FIELD rustc_code ON error_pattern TYPE option<string>;  -- E0505, E0106, etc.
DEFINE FIELD key_tokens ON error_pattern TYPE array<string>;  -- ['cannot borrow', 'mutable']
DEFINE FIELD original_code ON error_pattern TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD fix_diff ON error_pattern TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD strategy ON error_pattern TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD model_tier ON error_pattern TYPE string;  -- strand/hydra/behemoth/council
DEFINE FIELD iterations ON error_pattern TYPE int;
DEFINE FIELD success ON error_pattern TYPE bool;
DEFINE FIELD embedding ON error_pattern TYPE option<array<float>>;  -- for vector similarity
DEFINE FIELD created_at ON error_pattern TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();

-- Indexes
DEFINE INDEX idx_category ON error_pattern FIELDS category;
DEFINE INDEX idx_rustc_code ON error_pattern FIELDS rustc_code;
DEFINE INDEX idx_embedding ON error_pattern FIELDS embedding MTREE DIMENSION 384 DIST COSINE TYPE F32;

-- Archival Memory (Letta/MemGPT pattern)
DEFINE TABLE memory SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD bead_id ON memory TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD memory_type ON memory TYPE string;  -- diff, verifier_report, error, decision, note
DEFINE FIELD content ON memory TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD summary ON memory TYPE option<string>;
DEFINE FIELD outcome ON memory TYPE option<string>;  -- success, failed, partial
DEFINE FIELD priority ON memory TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
DEFINE FIELD embedding ON memory TYPE option<array<float>>;
DEFINE FIELD created_at ON memory TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();

DEFINE INDEX idx_bead ON memory FIELDS bead_id;
DEFINE INDEX idx_type ON memory FIELDS memory_type;
DEFINE INDEX idx_mem_embedding ON memory FIELDS embedding MTREE DIMENSION 384 DIST COSINE TYPE F32;

-- Graph edges: error → fixed_by → fix_strategy
DEFINE TABLE fixed_by SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD strategy_used ON fixed_by TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD confidence ON fixed_by TYPE float;

-- Model performance tracking
DEFINE TABLE model_stats SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD model_id ON model_stats TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD category ON model_stats TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD attempts ON model_stats TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
DEFINE FIELD successes ON model_stats TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
DEFINE FIELD avg_iterations ON model_stats TYPE float DEFAULT 0.0;
DEFINE FIELD updated_at ON model_stats TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();

DEFINE INDEX idx_model_cat ON model_stats FIELDS model_id, category UNIQUE;
```

### 8. Example Queries

```sql
-- Find similar error patterns (top 3 by vector similarity)
SELECT *, vector::similarity::cosine(embedding, $query_embedding) AS sim
FROM error_pattern
WHERE category = 'BorrowChecker'
  AND embedding <|3|> $query_embedding;

-- Model success rate by category (for routing decisions)
SELECT model_id, category, 
       (successes * 1.0 / attempts) AS success_rate,
       avg_iterations
FROM model_stats
WHERE attempts > 5
ORDER BY success_rate DESC;

-- Recall failed approaches for a bead (Letta pattern)
SELECT content, summary, outcome
FROM memory
WHERE bead_id = $current_bead
  AND outcome = 'failed'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Graph: what fixes worked for errors in similar crates?
SELECT ->fixed_by->error_pattern.strategy AS strategies
FROM error_pattern
WHERE category = $category
  AND rustc_code = $code;
```

### 9. Migration Path

1. **Phase 1 (now):** Deploy SurrealDB on ai-proxy (beefcake-swarm-3is.1.2)
2. **Phase 2:** Implement Error KB in SurrealDB (beefcake-swarm-3is.5.2) — new code, no migration
3. **Phase 3:** Implement Archival Memory in SurrealDB (beefcake-swarm-3is.5.4) — new code, no migration  
4. **Phase 4:** Keep existing RocksDB for ensemble coordination (no change to store.rs)
5. **Future:** If coordination needs multi-agent access, migrate remaining state to SurrealDB

### 10. Risk Mitigation

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|
| SurrealDB latency on hot path | Don't use it for hot path — keep direct RocksDB |
| SurrealDB instability (young project) | Isolated on ai-proxy, can fallback to direct RocksDB KB |
| Network failure (ai-proxy down) | Degrade gracefully — skip KB lookup, use empty playbooks |
| Embedding generation overhead | Use small model (384-dim) or hash-based signatures initially |
| Schema evolution | SurrealQL supports `DEFINE FIELD` additions without migration |

### 11. Conclusion

The hybrid approach gives us the best of both worlds:
- **Direct RocksDB** stays for what it does well: fast, simple, per-issue state (μs latency)
- **SurrealDB** handles what RocksDB can't: graph traversals, vector similarity, aggregations, multi-agent access, and the full UKG vision

Both beefcake-swarm-3r9 (Error KB) and beefcake-swarm-b30 (Archival Memory) should target SurrealDB, NOT add more column families to the existing RocksDB store. The existing store.rs remains untouched.

### Sources
- SurrealDB Architecture: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/introduction/architecture
- SurrealDB Graph Model: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/models/graph
- SurrealDB Vector Model: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/models/vector
- RELATE Statement: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/relate
- Performance Best Practices: https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/reference-guide/performance-best-practices
- Embedded SurrealDB Perf Issue: https://github.com/surrealdb/surrealdb/issues/4767
- SurrealDB Scalability: https://surrealdb.com/blog/surrealdb-scalability
- crud-bench: https://github.com/surrealdb/crud-bench
- MemGPT Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08560
- Letta/MemGPT Docs: https://docs.letta.com/concepts/memgpt/
- Long-Term Memory Patterns: https://serokell.io/blog/design-patterns-for-long-term-memory-in-llm-powered-architectures

notes:
PAL chat with g3-pro (Librarian). Output: feature matrix + config example. Check GitHub repo for SurrealDB native support vs needing a custom adapter.
## codegraph-rust Research Findings (2026-02-13)

### Overview
- **Repo**: github.com/Jakedismo/codegraph-rust (73 stars, 8 forks)
- **Language**: 100% Rust, multi-crate workspace
- **License**: Open source
- **Key Tech**: tree-sitter + FastML parsing, RocksDB + FAISS (primary), SurrealDB (experimental graph backend)

### Dual Storage Backends

**Primary: RocksDB + FAISS**
- Graph structure stored in RocksDB (embedded KV store)
- Vector similarity via FAISS
- High performance, no external DB dependency
- Supports incremental updates (reprocess only changed files)
- FAISS limitation: adding new vectors may require partial index rebuild

**Experimental: SurrealDB Graph Backend**
- File: `crates/codegraph-graph/src/surrealdb_storage.rs` (CONFIRMED EXISTS)
- Uses official SurrealDB Rust SDK (`Surreal<Any>` type)
- Connects via WebSocket (ws://localhost:3004 default)
- Namespace: "ouroboros", Database: "codegraph"
- Schema files: `schema/codegraph.surql` (relational), `schema/codegraph_graph_experimental.surql` (graph)
- Supports auth (username/password)
- Migrations: `crates/codegraph-graph/src/surrealdb_migrations.rs`

### SurrealDB Tables
| Table | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `nodes` | Code entities (functions, classes, methods, variables) |
| `edges` | Relationships (calls, imports, inherits, contains, defines, uses, flows_to, returns, mutates, extends, implements, references, depends_on, violates_boundary — ~20 types) |
| `chunks` | Text chunks linked to parent nodes |
| `file_metadata` | Per-file stats (content_hash, language, node_count, edge_count) |
| `symbol_embeddings` | Cached identifier-level embeddings |
| `project_metadata` | Project-level metadata |

### Embedding Support
- Multi-dimensional: 384, 768, 1024, 1536, 2048, 3072, 4096
- HNSW indexes on all embedding dimensions (DIST COSINE, EFC 100, M 16)
- Providers: Ollama, LM Studio, OpenAI, Jina AI
- Full-text search indexes on `content` and `name` fields

### Node Types
Function, Class, Method, Variable (core AST types from tree-sitter)

### Edge Types (~20 total)
calls, defines, imports, uses, extends, implements, references, contains, inherits, flows_to, returns, mutates, depends_on, violates_boundary, and more

### Indexing Tiers
| Tier | Features | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| fast | AST nodes, core edges only. Disables build context, LSP, enrichment, module linking, dataflow | Quick indexing, CI |
| balanced | + build context, LSP symbols, enrichment, module linking, docs/contracts | Development use |
| full | All analyzers, LSP definitions, dataflow, architecture analysis, no edge filtering | Complete analysis |

### MCP Tools (4 agentic tools)
1. **agentic_context** — Semantic search, context building, question answering. Focus: search|builder|question
2. **agentic_impact** — Dependency chains, call flows, change impact. Focus: dependencies|call_chain
3. **agentic_architecture** — System structure, API surfaces, architectural patterns. Focus: structure|api_surface
4. **agentic_quality** — Complexity hotspots, coupling metrics, refactoring priorities. Focus: complexity|coupling|hotspots

All tools run reasoning agents internally (plan → search → analyze graph → synthesize).

### SurrealDB Graph Functions
- `semantic_search_nodes_via_chunks` — Vector search over code chunks
- `get_transitive_dependencies` — Transitive dependency resolution
- `trace_call_chain` — Call chain tracing
- `calculate_coupling_metrics` — Module coupling analysis

### Performance Reference
- Sample run: 1,505 files, 2.4M lines, 30K functions, 880 classes, 539K embeddings
- HNSW query: 2-5ms
- Supports 14 languages via tree-sitter: Rust, Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, Java, C++, C, Swift, Kotlin, C#, Ruby, PHP, Dart

### Agent Architecture
- Uses **Rig** framework (same as our swarm-agents crate!)
- Reasoning strategies: LATS (tree search), ReAct (linear), Reflexion (self-correcting)
- Context-adaptive: adjusts depth based on LLM context window size

### Assessment: Fork Needed?
**Likely NO fork needed** — codegraph-rust already has:
- ✅ Native SurrealDB storage backend (`surrealdb_storage.rs`)
- ✅ SurrealDB graph experimental schema
- ✅ Multi-dimensional embeddings compatible with our Nomic 3584-dim (closest: 4096)
- ✅ Rust language with advanced analysis
- ✅ Rig framework (same as our codebase)
- ✅ MCP server for tool exposure
- ✅ Incremental indexing

**Potential customization points (config, not fork):**
- Embedding dimension: may need to add 3584 HNSW index to schema (currently supports 384-4096)
- SurrealDB connection config: point to our SurrealDB instance
- Indexing tier: use "full" for ~21K LOC codebase (small enough)
- Could run as MCP server alongside our coordination MCP

**Risk factors:**
- SurrealDB backend is labeled "experimental"
- 73 stars = small community, may need to contribute fixes upstream
- FAISS incremental updates have limitations (partial rebuild needed)
- No published benchmarks for Rust-specific analysis quality

## CocoIndex Custom SurrealDB Target — Research Findings (2026-02-13)

### Current Pipeline Analysis
- File: `indexing/index_flow_v2.py`
- Pattern: `@flow_def(name="code_indexing")` with `FlowBuilder` + `DataScope`
- Source: `LocalFile` from rust-daq repo
- Transform: `DetectProgrammingLanguage` → `SplitRecursively` (1000 chars) → `EmbedText` (Nomic 3584-dim via vasp-02)
- Target: `Postgres` with pgvector HNSW index (cosine similarity)
- Live updates: 30-second refresh interval
- Query handler: `semantic_search` over `code_chunks` table

### COCOINDEX_GRAPH_RAG.md Assessment
**OUTDATED** — The document references `cocoindex.typing.Target` and `batch_write()` method, which do NOT match the current CocoIndex v1 API. The actual API uses:
- `cocoindex.op.TargetSpec` (not `cocoindex.typing.Target`)
- `@cocoindex.op.target_connector(spec_cls=...)` decorator
- `mutate()` method (not `batch_write()`)
- `apply_setup_change()` for infrastructure lifecycle
- `get_persistent_key()` for target identity

### CocoIndex v1 Custom Target API (Validated)

#### 1. TargetSpec (Configuration)
```python
class SurrealDBTarget(cocoindex.op.TargetSpec):
    url: str           # ws://localhost:8000
    namespace: str     # "ouroboros"
    database: str      # "codegraph"
    table_name: str    # "doc_chunks"
    auth_ref: cocoindex.AuthEntryReference | None = None
```

#### 2. TargetConnector (Implementation)
```python
@cocoindex.op.target_connector(spec_cls=SurrealDBTarget)
class SurrealDBTargetConnector:
    @staticmethod
    def get_persistent_key(spec, target_name) -> str:
        return f"{spec.url}/{spec.namespace}/{spec.database}/{spec.table_name}"

    @staticmethod
    def apply_setup_change(key, previous, current):
        # Create/delete SurrealDB table + HNSW indexes
        if previous is None and current is not None:
            # CREATE TABLE, DEFINE INDEX for embeddings
            pass
        if previous is not None and current is None:
            # DROP TABLE
            pass

    @staticmethod
    def mutate(*all_mutations):
        for spec, mutations in all_mutations:
            for key, value in mutations.items():
                if value is None:
                    # DELETE chunk:key
                    pass
                else:
                    # UPSERT chunk:key CONTENT {...}
                    pass
```

#### 3. Key Methods
| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `get_persistent_key()` | Returns unique identifier for target instance |
| `apply_setup_change()` | Creates/destroys target infrastructure (tables, indexes) |
| `mutate()` | Applies data changes (insert/update/delete) — REQUIRED |
| `prepare()` | Optional: pre-connect, validate, cache connections |
| `describe()` | Optional: logging/debugging |

### Dual-Write Architecture: CONFIRMED POSSIBLE

CocoIndex supports multiple collectors and multiple export() calls in a single flow. Two approaches:

#### Option A: Two Collectors (Recommended)
```python
@flow_def(name="code_indexing")
def my_flow(builder, scope):
    files = builder.add_source(LocalFile(...))
    row = files.row()
    row["language"] = row["filename"].transform(DetectProgrammingLanguage())
    row["chunks"] = row["content"].transform(SplitRecursively(), ...)
    chunks = row["chunks"].row()
    chunks["embedding"] = text_to_embedding(chunks["text"])

    # Collector 1: Postgres (existing)
    pg_collector = scope.add_collector()
    pg_collector.collect(filename=row["filename"], ...)
    pg_collector.export("postgres_export", Postgres(...), ...)

    # Collector 2: SurrealDB (new)
    surreal_collector = scope.add_collector()
    surreal_collector.collect(filename=row["filename"], ...)
    surreal_collector.export("surreal_export", SurrealDBTarget(...), ...)
```

#### Option B: Single Collector, Two Exports
May also work — two export() calls from same collector. Less documented but architecturally plausible.

### SurrealDB Python SDK
- Package: `surrealdb` on PyPI (v1.0.8, Jan 2026)
- Protocols: HTTP, WebSocket, embedded
- Async + sync support
- CBOR serialization (binary, efficient)
- Compatible: SurrealDB v2.0.0 — v2.3.6
- Mature: 227 tests, 100% coverage, Python 3.9-3.13

### Built-in Target Precedent
CocoIndex already has a **Neo4j** target (graph database), proving graph DB targets are first-class citizens. The SurrealDB target follows the same pattern:
- Nodes → `collector.export(Neo4j(mapping=Nodes(label="...")))`
- Could do similar: `collector.export(SurrealDBTarget(table_name="doc_chunks"))`

### Implementation Approach (Recommended)

1. **Create `indexing/targets/surreal_target.py`** — Custom TargetSpec + Connector
2. **SurrealDB connection**: Use `surrealdb` Python SDK with WebSocket
3. **Schema**: `apply_setup_change()` creates table with HNSW vector indexes matching codegraph-rust schema (embedding_3584 field, COSINE distance)
4. **Mutations**: `mutate()` does UPSERT/DELETE via SurrealQL parameterized queries
5. **Dual-write**: Add second collector to existing flow in `index_flow_v2.py`
6. **Bridge edges**: Post-process to create `documents` edges between doc chunks and code nodes (from codegraph-rust)

### Schema Alignment with codegraph-rust
The SurrealDB target should write to tables compatible with codegraph-rust's experimental schema:
- Table: `chunks` (matching codegraph-rust's chunk table)
- Fields: `project_id`, `parent_node`, `text`, `embedding_3584` (new dimension)
- Or separate table: `doc_chunks` to avoid collision with code chunks

### Risk Assessment
| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| SurrealDB Python SDK maturity | Stable v1.0.8, well-tested |
| Custom Target API stability | CocoIndex v1 API, blog + docs consistent |
| Dual-write performance | Async SurrealDB writes, CocoIndex handles batching |
| Schema collision with codegraph-rust | Use separate table (`doc_chunks`) or namespace |
| 3584-dim not in codegraph schema | Add custom HNSW index definition |

### Conclusion
**Dual-write is fully supported and straightforward.** The custom target requires ~100 lines of Python. The existing pipeline needs minimal modification (add second collector + export). No fork of CocoIndex needed — the custom target API is designed for exactly this use case.

## COMPREHENSIVE RESEARCH: Self-Learning Architectures for Coding Agents

### Research Date: 2026-02-13
### Methodology: Web search + deep dives on 7 systems + SurrealDB graph patterns + episodic memory literature

---

## 1. COMPARISON MATRIX

| System | What Gets Stored | Retrieval Method | Success Signal | Bad Pattern Prevention | Storage Backend |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| SWE-agent (SWE-Bench-CL) | Problem summaries, solutions, rationales, tool usage, success status | FAISS vector index, cosine similarity on embeddings (text-embedding-3-small), top-k retrieval | Task completion status, forward/backward knowledge transfer metrics, CL-Plasticity/Stability | Success status tracked per memory; low inter-task similarity limits spurious transfer | FAISS vector store |
| OpenHands | Agent orchestration state, task decomposition patterns, CI/CD pipeline results | REST API with sandboxed runtimes, human feedback loop | Human verification at checkpoints, CI/CD pipeline pass | 90/10 automation/human split; human oversight on strategy | In-memory + external integrations |
| Goose (Block) | Recipes (YAML workflow definitions) with goals, required extensions, structured inputs, sub-recipes | MCP-based tool discovery, recipe matching | Recipe execution success, tool chain completion | Open-source community review; recipe versioning | File-based YAML recipes, versioned in git |
| Cursor Composer | Project rules (.mdc files), user rules, team rules, agent rules (AGENTS.md) | Codebase-wide semantic search, self-summarization for long contexts | RL training signal (test pass, linter pass), reinforcement learning at scale | RL training in real codebases filters low-quality patterns naturally | Rules files in .cursor/rules/, MoE model weights |
| Aider | Git commit history with descriptive messages, conversation context | Git-integrated diff history, /run command for test feedback | Test execution results, compilation success, git commit success | Git-based rollback; human review of each commit | Git repository (commit history as memory) |
| DSPy | Optimized prompts, few-shot examples, bootstrap demonstrations | BootstrapFewShot selection, MIPROv2 instruction generation, GEPA (Genetic-Pareto) evolution | Metric-driven evaluation (user-defined), feedback loops | Metric quality bounds bad learning; GEPA reflective optimization allows course correction | In-memory pipeline parameters, serialized optimized programs |
| Letta/MemGPT | Core memory blocks (in-context), archival memory (explicit knowledge), recall memory (conversation history), skills (.md files) | Vector search, full-text search, hybrid search across messages/tools; graph traversal | Task completion, user satisfaction, memory relevance | Sleep-time agents for async memory refinement; memory blocks with explicit constraints/limits | Postgres (default), SQLite, vector indexes, git-versioned skills |

---

## 2. DETAILED SYSTEM ANALYSES

### 2.1 SWE-agent / SWE-Bench-CL (Princeton)

**Architecture:** Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) design. The SWE-Bench-CL paper introduces continual learning where agents vectorize task experiences.

**Storage Details:**
- After each task: problem summary, solution approach, rationale, tool usage patterns, and success/failure status are vectorized
- Uses OpenAI text-embedding-3-small for embeddings
- FAISS vector index for storage and retrieval

**Retrieval:**
- On new task: query memory with current problem statement
- Cosine similarity ranking
- Top-k memories prepended to agent's initial prompt
- Prioritizes experiences from same task sequence

**Key Insight:** Low inter-task structural similarity is a major challenge. Contextual sensitivity ("prompt poisoning") means retrieved memories can hurt as much as help if not well-filtered.

**Metrics:** CL-Plasticity (ability to learn new), CL-Stability (retention of old), forward/backward knowledge transfer.

### 2.2 OpenHands (All Hands AI)

**Architecture:** Multi-agent orchestration platform. Agents operate in sandboxed Docker environments.

**Learning Model:** Primarily relies on human-in-the-loop rather than automated self-learning. The platform targets 90% automation / 10% human effort, with humans providing strategy and verification at checkpoints.

**Key Insight:** OpenHands' value is in orchestration (coordinating agent fleets for large refactors), not in autonomous self-learning. Learning happens implicitly through the human feedback loop and agent orchestration patterns.

### 2.3 Goose (Block)

**Architecture:** MCP-based extensible agent framework. Uses "recipes" for reusable workflows.

**Storage:** Recipes are YAML workflow definitions containing:
- Goals
- Required MCP extensions
- Structured inputs
- Sub-recipes (composable workflows)
- Shared via git repositories

**Learning:** Recipes represent manually curated "learned" patterns. No automated self-learning from past fixes. Instead, teams share and version recipes.

**Key Insight:** Recipe-based approach is deterministic and auditable but doesn't learn autonomously. Good model for encoding expert knowledge (human writes recipe once, agents reuse forever).

### 2.4 Cursor Composer

**Architecture:** Custom MoE model trained via reinforcement learning in real codebases.

**Learning:**
- Model trained in agentic setting with access to semantic search + test runners
- RL training teaches practical behaviors: running tests, fixing linters, navigating large projects
- Self-summarization for long context management
- Rules system for persistent project/user/team preferences

**Key Insight:** Cursor's "learning" is baked into model weights via RL, not retrievable patterns. The Rules system (.cursor/rules/*.mdc) is the closest to explicit pattern storage, but requires manual authoring.

### 2.5 Aider

**Architecture:** Terminal-based pair programming assistant with deep git integration.

**Learning:** Git-centric implicit learning:
- Auto-commits with descriptive messages create an audit trail
- /run command feeds test output back for iterative fixing
- Architect mode → Code mode → Ask mode workflow
- No explicit pattern storage or retrieval

**Key Insight:** Aider's "memory" IS the git history. Clean commit messages serve as a form of episodic memory. The /run feedback loop (run tests → see errors → fix → repeat) is a lightweight self-learning mechanism but doesn't persist across sessions.

### 2.6 DSPy (Stanford)

**Architecture:** Declarative framework for programming LMs. Key innovation: programs (not prompts) that self-optimize.

**Storage:**
- Optimized few-shot examples (bootstrapped from training data)
- Optimized instruction strings
- Pipeline parameters (serializable)

**Optimizers:**
- BootstrapFewShot: Finds most effective training examples
- MIPROv2: Generates optimized prompt instructions
- GEPA (2025): Genetic-Pareto reflective optimizer that evolves textual components

**Feedback Loop:**
1. Define metric (e.g., test pass rate)
2. Optimizer generates candidate prompts/examples
3. Evaluate against metric
4. Select best performers
5. Iterate (can achieve 25-65% improvement over standard few-shot)

**Key Insight:** DSPy is the most rigorous self-learning framework. Its metric-driven approach prevents bad learning naturally (bad patterns score low). However, it optimizes prompts/examples, not stored fix patterns per se. **Most applicable to beefcake-swarm:** DSPy's feedback loop model could be adapted to optimize how work packets are constructed.

### 2.7 Letta/MemGPT (UC Berkeley)

**Architecture:** Most sophisticated memory system. Three-tier OS-inspired memory hierarchy:

**Tier 1 - Core Memory (RAM equivalent):**
- In-context memory blocks with labels, descriptions, values, character limits
- Always visible to the agent
- Agent can self-edit these blocks
- Used for: current user info, agent persona, active task context

**Tier 2 - Recall Memory (Conversation History):**
- Complete interaction history
- Searchable via vector, full-text, or hybrid search
- Supports pagination for large histories

**Tier 3 - Archival Memory (Disk equivalent):**
- Explicitly stored processed knowledge
- Agent autonomously decides what to archive
- Vector-indexed for semantic retrieval
- No size limit

**Additional: Skills (.md files):**
- Learned task patterns stored as markdown
- Versioned in git
- Shareable between agents
- Captures repeated patterns (DB migrations, API changes)

**Memory Management:**
- Sleep-time agents: async processes that refine/consolidate memory
- Recursive summarization for context compression
- Eviction strategies for less relevant tokens
- Git-based versioning of agent memories

**Key Insight:** Letta's architecture is the most directly applicable to beefcake-swarm. The tiered memory model maps naturally to: Core Memory = current work packet context, Recall Memory = past fix attempts for this issue, Archival Memory = cross-issue pattern knowledge base.

---

## 3. SURREALDB-SPECIFIC PATTERNS FOR FIX STORAGE

### 3.1 Recommended Schema

```surql
-- Error pattern node
DEFINE TABLE error_pattern SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD error_code ON error_pattern TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD error_category ON error_pattern TYPE string;  -- borrow_checker, lifetime, trait_bounds, type_mismatch, async_send
DEFINE FIELD error_signature ON error_pattern TYPE string;  -- normalized error message
DEFINE FIELD embedding ON error_pattern TYPE array<float>;  -- vector embedding of error context
DEFINE FIELD frequency ON error_pattern TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
DEFINE FIELD first_seen ON error_pattern TYPE datetime;
DEFINE FIELD last_seen ON error_pattern TYPE datetime;

-- Fix strategy node
DEFINE TABLE fix_strategy SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD description ON fix_strategy TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD diff_template ON fix_strategy TYPE string;  -- generalized diff pattern
DEFINE FIELD model_tier ON fix_strategy TYPE string;  -- which model tier produced this fix
DEFINE FIELD confidence ON fix_strategy TYPE float;  -- 0.0-1.0 based on success rate
DEFINE FIELD embedding ON fix_strategy TYPE array<float>;
DEFINE FIELD times_applied ON fix_strategy TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
DEFINE FIELD times_succeeded ON fix_strategy TYPE int DEFAULT 0;

-- File context node
DEFINE TABLE file_context SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD file_path ON file_context TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD crate_name ON file_context TYPE string;
DEFINE FIELD key_symbols ON file_context TYPE array<string>;

-- Relationships (graph edges)
-- error_pattern -[FIXED_BY]-> fix_strategy
DEFINE TABLE fixed_by SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD success_rate ON fixed_by TYPE float;
DEFINE FIELD last_applied ON fixed_by TYPE datetime;

-- error_pattern -[OCCURS_IN]-> file_context
DEFINE TABLE occurs_in SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD frequency ON occurs_in TYPE int;

-- fix_strategy -[MODIFIES]-> file_context
DEFINE TABLE modifies SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD change_type ON modifies TYPE string;  -- add, remove, modify

-- fix_strategy -[ESCALATED_FROM]-> fix_strategy
DEFINE TABLE escalated_from SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD reason ON escalated_from TYPE string;

-- error_pattern -[CO_OCCURS_WITH]-> error_pattern
DEFINE TABLE co_occurs_with SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD frequency ON co_occurs_with TYPE int;
```

### 3.2 Retrieval Strategy: Hybrid (Graph + Vector)

**Primary Query Pattern (Concept-Based):**
1. Embed the current error context
2. Vector search on error_pattern.embedding for top-5 similar errors
3. Graph traverse: error_pattern -[FIXED_BY]-> fix_strategy (filter by confidence > 0.7)
4. Rank fix strategies by: (semantic_similarity * 0.4) + (confidence * 0.3) + (recency * 0.2) + (model_tier_match * 0.1)

```surql
-- Example hybrid query
LET $query_embedding = <embedding from current error>;
LET $similar_errors = (
    SELECT *, embedding <|5,40|> $query_embedding AS similarity
    FROM error_pattern
    WHERE error_category = $current_category
);
LET $fix_candidates = (
    SELECT <-fixed_by<-fix_strategy.* AS strategy, 
           <-fixed_by.success_rate AS success_rate
    FROM $similar_errors
    WHERE <-fixed_by.success_rate > 0.7
    ORDER BY success_rate DESC
);
```

**Secondary Pattern (Co-occurrence Graph Walk):**
When multiple errors appear together (common in Rust cascade failures):
1. Find current error in graph
2. Walk co_occurs_with edges to find related errors
3. Find fixes that address the ROOT error (not the cascade)
4. This prevents applying band-aid fixes to symptom errors

### 3.3 Graph Traversal vs Vector Search vs Hybrid

| Approach | Best For | Weakness |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Pure Vector | Novel errors never seen before | Misses structural relationships (A always follows B) |
| Pure Graph | Known error cascades, dependency chains | Can't handle novel errors |
| Hybrid (RECOMMENDED) | Both known and novel patterns | Slightly more complex queries |

**Recommendation:** Start with hybrid. Vector search handles cold start and novel errors. Graph traversal improves as the KB grows and captures structural patterns the vector space misses.

---

## 4. COLD START / BOOTSTRAP STRATEGY

### 4.1 Phase 1: Seed from Existing Data
1. **Mine git history:** Parse past commits for error→fix pairs. Use `git log --all -p` + rustc error parsing to extract (error_signature, diff, success=true) tuples
2. **Mine beads notes:** Extract error descriptions and resolution notes from closed issues
3. **Import SWE-Bench patterns:** Use published SWE-Bench fix patterns as initial seed (Rust-specific subset)

### 4.2 Phase 2: Active Learning
1. Every verifier pass/fail generates a (error_context, fix_attempt, outcome) tuple
2. On success: create/update error_pattern → fix_strategy edge with incremented success count
3. On failure: create/update edge with failure count, potentially create escalated_from link

### 4.3 Phase 3: Consolidation
1. Run periodic "sleep-time" consolidation (borrowing from Letta):
   - Merge similar error_pattern nodes (cosine similarity > 0.95)
   - Prune fix_strategy nodes with success_rate < 0.1 and times_applied > 5
   - Generalize diff_templates from specific to abstract patterns

### 4.4 Cold Start Mitigations
- **Content-based fallback:** If KB has no relevant patterns, fall back to error_category-based heuristics (the router already classifies errors by type)
- **Model-as-prior:** Use the LLM's pretrained knowledge as the initial "memory" until the KB builds up
- **Transfer from documentation:** Seed KB with Rust compiler error explanations (rustc --explain EXXXX) mapped to common fix strategies

---

## 5. RETENTION / DECAY POLICIES

### 5.1 Recommended Policy
```
Confidence decay: confidence *= 0.95 per week if not revalidated
Minimum threshold: Remove patterns with confidence < 0.1 after 30 days
Frequency boost: Recently-used patterns get confidence += 0.05 per successful application
Maximum age: Patterns not used in 90 days get flagged for review
Never delete: Patterns with success_rate > 0.9 and times_applied > 10 (these are "golden rules")
```

### 5.2 Preventing Bad Pattern Learning
1. **Minimum evidence threshold:** Require times_applied >= 3 before a fix_strategy's confidence exceeds 0.5
2. **Blind validation:** The validator agent (14B, no implementer context) independently verifies fixes - only validated fixes update confidence positively
3. **Cascade detection:** If a fix introduces new errors, decrement confidence by 0.2 and create a co_occurs_with edge to the new error
4. **Human veto:** Escalated-to-human fixes automatically get confidence capped at 0.3 until human approves
5. **A/B testing:** Periodically apply KB-suggested fix vs. fresh LLM attempt on same error; only keep KB pattern if it outperforms

---

## 6. RECOMMENDED ARCHITECTURE FOR BEEFCAKE-SWARM

### 6.1 Architecture: Hybrid Letta-Inspired + DSPy-Optimized + SurrealDB Graph

**Layer 1: Working Memory (Work Packet)**
- Current error context, file contexts, key symbols
- Maps to Letta's "Core Memory"
- Already implemented in work_packet/ module

**Layer 2: Episodic Memory (SurrealDB Graph)**
- Error patterns, fix strategies, relationships
- Graph structure enables cascade detection and co-occurrence analysis
- Vector embeddings enable novel error matching
- Maps to Letta's "Archival Memory"

**Layer 3: Strategic Memory (DSPy-optimized prompts)**
- Optimized prompt templates per error category
- Few-shot examples that have been validated as effective
- Periodically re-optimized using DSPy's BootstrapFewShot against test suite metrics
- Maps to Letta's "Recall Memory" but more structured

**Feedback Loop (DSPy-inspired):**
1. Error occurs → query Layer 2 (SurrealDB) for similar patterns
2. If match found: inject fix strategy into work packet
3. If no match: use Layer 3 optimized prompts for error category
4. If still failing: escalate per existing escalation ladder
5. After resolution: update Layer 2 with new evidence
6. Periodically: re-optimize Layer 3 prompts using accumulated evidence

### 6.2 Integration Points with Existing Codebase

| Existing Module | Integration |
|----------------|-------------|
| verifier/ | Generates (error, outcome) tuples → feeds Layer 2 |
| escalation/ | Queries Layer 2 before escalating; uses past fix data to skip unnecessary tiers |
| router/ | Uses Layer 2 error_category statistics to improve routing accuracy |
| work_packet/ | Enriched with Layer 2 fix suggestions before sending to model |
| feedback/ | Closes the loop: compilation results update Layer 2 confidence scores |
| ensemble/ | Multi-model voting results stored as evidence in Layer 2 |

### 6.3 Implementation Priority
1. SurrealDB schema + basic CRUD for error_pattern and fix_strategy
2. Integration with verifier output parser (error → pattern extraction)
3. Vector embedding pipeline (embed error contexts)
4. Work packet enrichment (query KB before sending to model)
5. Confidence update loop (success/failure feedback)
6. DSPy prompt optimization layer (requires accumulated data)
7. Sleep-time consolidation agent (periodic cleanup)

---

## 7. KEY TAKEAWAYS

1. **No existing system does exactly what beefcake-swarm needs.** SWE-agent has the closest retrieval architecture (vector-based episodic memory), but lacks graph structure for error cascades. Letta has the best memory hierarchy, but isn't code-fix-specific. DSPy has the best feedback loop, but optimizes prompts not fix patterns.

2. **The hybrid approach is novel and valuable.** Combining graph structure (for Rust error cascades, which are inherently relational) with vector search (for novel errors) with DSPy-style metric optimization (for prompt quality) creates a system more capable than any single existing approach.

3. **SurrealDB is a strong fit** because it natively supports document + graph + vector in one database, eliminating the need to sync between a vector DB and a graph DB.

4. **The cold start problem is solvable** by mining existing git history and beads notes, plus using the Rust compiler's own error explanations as seed data.

5. **Blind validation (existing validator agent) is a critical anti-bad-learning mechanism** that most other systems lack. The beefcake-swarm architecture already has this with the independent 14B validator.

description:
The Implementer and Validator create rig-core CompletionsClient instances without configuring timeouts. If the inference server hangs (GPU OOM, SLURM preemption, network partition), the HTTP request blocks indefinitely and the orchestrator loop stalls.

Fix: Check rig-core's client builder for timeout configuration:
1. Set connect_timeout (5s) and request_timeout (5min for 72B reasoning, 2min for 14B fast)
2. Add these as fields in SwarmConfig per endpoint
3. If rig-core doesn't support timeouts directly, wrap the implement/validate calls with tokio::time::timeout()

Also consider: retry logic for transient HTTP failures (502, 503 from inference server starting up).

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/implementer.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/validator.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/config.rs
Found by: G3-Pro deep review
title: Configure rig-core client timeouts

close_reason: Stale — references main.rs:253/217 which no longer exist. Current orchestrator.rs uses last_report to carry verifier results forward between iterations. No redundant verifier runs.
description:
The orchestrator loop in main.rs runs the verifier TWICE per retry iteration: once at the end of iteration N (line 253) to check implementer output, then again at the start of iteration N+1 (line 217) to build retry context for pack_retry(). Rust compilation is expensive (~seconds per run); doubling it wastes time and cluster resources.

Fix: Carry the VerifierReport forward from the end of each loop iteration into the next via Option<VerifierReport>. Only run the verifier once per iteration. The report from the failed iteration already contains everything pack_retry() needs.

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/main.rs
Found by: G3-Pro deep review
title: Fix redundant verifier run in orchestrator loop
