description:
UNANIMOUS CONSENSUS (4/4 models)

The swarm currently has NO memory across issues. EscalationState tracks error history for one issue only. The same borrow checker error pattern might be solved 50 times without the swarm ever learning the fix.

Implement a persistent Error Pattern KB:
1. After each successful fix, store: (error_signature, original_code_snippet, fix_diff, strategy_description, model_tier, iterations_to_resolve)
2. Before each implementation attempt, query the KB for similar past errors (match on ErrorCategory + key tokens from message + file structure)
3. Include top-3 matching successful strategies in WorkPacket.relevant_playbooks (field already exists but is always empty)

Storage: Use the existing RocksDB infrastructure in coordination/state/. Add a new column family for fix patterns.

Error signature should include: ErrorCategory, rustc error code, key tokens from the error message (e.g. 'cannot borrow', 'lifetime mismatch'), affected symbol types.

This is essentially few-shot learning from the swarm's own history. G3-Pro calls it 'Compiler RAG'. Opus calls it 'the missing flywheel'.

Also track per-model success rates by error category to inform routing decisions (e.g. if 14B has <40% success on Lifetime errors, escalate immediately).

Files: coordination/src/state/ (new KB module), coordination/src/context_packer/packer.rs, coordination/src/work_packet/generator.rs
title: Build Error Pattern Knowledge Base for cross-issue learning

close_reason: Premature — revisit after 50+ completions
description:
DSRs (https://dsrs.herumbshandilya.com/) is a Rust-native rewrite of the DSPy framework for programming robust LM-powered applications. It provides typed Signatures, composable Modules, Predictors, and Optimizers (COPRO, MIPROv2, GEPA) for automatically improving LLM prompts and pipelines.

Critically, DSRs already uses rig-core as its LM backend — the same crate swarm-agents uses for Implementer and Validator. This means integration could be relatively seamless.

Key opportunities for beefcake-swarm:

1. **Signature-based prompting**: Replace the hand-rolled format_work_packet() prompt builder with typed DSRs Signatures. This gives structured input/output contracts that the framework can optimize automatically.

2. **Prompt optimization**: Use DSRs optimizers to automatically tune the implementer and validator prompts. Instead of manually iterating on prompt wording, COPRO/MIPROv2 can search for better prompts given a set of examples and a metric (e.g. verifier pass rate).

3. **Evaluation framework**: DSRs' evaluate module could replace or augment the validator's pass/fail heuristic (starts_with PASS) with a more robust evaluation pipeline.

4. **Pipeline composition**: The swarm loop (pack -> implement -> verify -> validate) maps naturally to DSRs' Module composition pattern, potentially simplifying the orchestrator.

Investigation steps:
1. Add dspy-rs = "0.7" to swarm-agents/Cargo.toml
2. Define Signatures for the implementer task (input: WorkPacket fields, output: code changes) and validator task (input: diff, output: pass/fail + feedback)
3. Wrap Implementer and Validator as DSRs Modules
4. Evaluate whether DSRs Predictors can replace the raw rig-core prompt() calls
5. Prototype an optimizer run on a set of known-good/bad code changes to auto-tune prompts

Risks:
- DSRs is in beta (v0.7.3) — API may have breaking changes
- Optimizer training requires a dataset of examples with ground truth
- May add significant dependency weight (arrow, parquet deps)
- Unclear if DSRs supports local llama.cpp endpoints natively (needs OpenAI-compatible API adapter)

Reference: https://github.com/krypticmouse/DSRs, https://crates.io/crates/dspy-rs, https://docs.rs/dspy-rs

Depends on: agent traits extraction (needed to wrap agents as DSRs Modules)
title: Evaluate DSRs (dspy-rs) for structured LLM pipeline optimization

description:
FileWalker::modified_files() and WorktreeBridge merge checks parse 'git status --porcelain' output by slicing at [3..]. While porcelain v1 format is stable for normal cases, renamed files produce 'R  old -> new' format, and filenames containing spaces or special characters can cause incorrect parsing.

Fix: Switch to 'git status -z --porcelain' which uses NUL byte separators instead of newlines, handles renames as two separate NUL-separated entries, and correctly handles filenames with spaces, quotes, and unicode characters. Parse by splitting on \0 instead of \n.

Files: coordination/src/context_packer/file_walker.rs, coordination/src/work_packet/generator.rs
Found by: G3-Pro deep review
title: Use git status -z for robust porcelain parsing

description:
UNIQUE INSIGHT from GPT-5.2-Codex and Opus

Replace flat 'previous_attempts' strings with structured iteration deltas that capture WHAT CHANGED between iterations, not just what happened.

Add IterationDelta struct:
- fixed_errors: Vec<ErrorCategory> — what improved
- new_errors: Vec<ErrorCategory> — what regressed  
- files_modified: Vec<String> — what was touched
- hypothesis: Option<String> — what the model claimed it was doing (extracted from response)
- result_summary: String — 'borrow error fixed, but introduced lifetime error in return type'
- strategy_used: String — 'added Arc wrapper' or 'changed lifetime annotation'

Include only the last 2-3 deltas in context (not full history). The model needs to see 'you fixed X but broke Y' not 'here is everything that ever went wrong'.

Also add provenance tags to FileContext (source: compiler_error, diff, dependency, import) and decay: reduce weight of previously included context not re-referenced by new errors.

description:
Add 3 new Rig tools for worker agents in crates/swarm-agents/src/tools/:

TOOL 1: search_code_tool.rs — Wraps 'rg' (ripgrep) for code search
- Struct: SearchCodeTool { working_dir: PathBuf }
- Input: SearchCodeInput { pattern: String, glob: Option<String>, max_results: Option<usize> }
- Behavior: Runs 'rg --json <pattern> [--glob <glob>]' inside working_dir, parses JSON output, returns up to max_results (default 20) matches formatted as 'file:line: content'
- Sandbox: All results must be within working_dir (use sandbox_check on each file match)
- Follow exec_tool.rs pattern for Command execution with timeout

TOOL 2: file_exists_tool.rs — Check if files/directories exist
- Struct: FileExistsTool { working_dir: PathBuf }  
- Input: FileExistsInput { paths: Vec<String> }
- Behavior: For each path, sandbox_check then check existence. Return JSON object mapping path -> bool.
- Simple, no subprocess needed

TOOL 3: cargo_metadata_tool.rs — Expose workspace structure
- Struct: CargoMetadataTool { working_dir: PathBuf }
- Input: CargoMetadataInput {} (no args needed)
- Behavior: Run 'cargo metadata --format-version=1 --no-deps' in working_dir. Parse JSON, extract: workspace_root, package names, package paths, and per-package targets (lib/bin/test names). Return a compact summary (not full metadata JSON).

REGISTRATION: Add 'pub mod search_code_tool; pub mod file_exists_tool; pub mod cargo_metadata_tool;' to tools/mod.rs

All tools implement rig::tool::Tool with #[derive(Deserialize, Serialize, Debug)] on inputs and #[derive(thiserror::Error, Debug)] for errors.

description:
STRONG CONSENSUS (3/4 models)

Before merging, a dedicated agent should actively try to BREAK the implementation by generating edge-case tests, weird inputs, concurrency interleavings, and invalid states.

The SwarmTier::Adversary already exists in escalation/state.rs but isn't integrated into the loop.

Implementation:
1. After Verifier passes (all green), invoke the Adversary agent
2. Give it ONLY the diff and public API signatures (no implementation context — truly adversarial)
3. It generates: proptest harnesses, edge-case unit tests, boundary condition checks
4. Run these generated tests through the Verifier
5. If any Adversary test fails, reject the implementation and feed the failing test back to the Implementer

This catches 'compiles but is wrong' — the biggest failure mode that compilation gates miss.

G3-Pro: 'Active Sabotage / Mutation Testing'. Opus: 'Adversary actively tries to break the code'.

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/ (new adversary module), crates/swarm-agents/src/main.rs
title: Add Adversarial Breaker agent for pre-merge red-teaming

close_reason: Implemented cleanup_stale() in WorktreeBridge — prunes worktree bookkeeping, deletes orphaned swarm/* branches. Called on startup in main.rs. 2 tests added.
description:
If the orchestrator crashes mid-loop, /tmp may clean the worktree directory but the swarm/<issue_id> branch persists in the repo. On next run, git worktree add -b fails with 'A branch named swarm/... already exists' and the agent gets permanently stuck on that issue.

Fix: On startup (before the main loop), scan for orphaned swarm/* branches that have no corresponding worktree directory. For each orphan: attempt git branch -D to delete it. Also check git worktree list --porcelain for stale entries and run git worktree prune. This requires the remove_worktree() method from the worktree cleanup issue.

Add a WorktreeBridge::cleanup_stale() method that:
1. Runs git worktree prune
2. Lists all branches matching swarm/*
3. For each, checks if worktree_path(id) exists
4. If not, force-deletes the branch

Call cleanup_stale() in main() before entering the issue-picking loop.

Depends on: worktree cleanup method (beefcake-swarm-rb2)

close_reason: Implemented try_auto_fix() Janitor layer: clippy --fix + fmt before LLM delegation
description:
After job 1621: the orchestrator spent 45+ minutes delegating a missing-paren syntax error to the cloud manager, which then delegated to a local worker, which then needed to read the file, understand the error, write the fix — all for a single character.

IMPLEMENTATION:
After verifier fails and before routing to the manager, check if errors are "trivially auto-fixable":

1. **Syntax errors with compiler suggestions**: If rustc provides a `suggested_replacement` in the diagnostic spans, apply it directly via string replacement.

2. **Format-only failures**: If fmt is the only failing gate and the file parses (no syntax errors), just run `cargo fmt --` on the file (already happens in auto-format, but currently blocked by syntax errors).

3. **Missing delimiters**: Parse the fmt/check error for patterns like "mismatched closing delimiter" or "unclosed delimiter" with line/column info. For simple cases (missing `)`, `}`, `]`), insert the character at the indicated position.

Add this as a `try_auto_fix()` method in the orchestrator that runs between verifier failure and manager delegation. If auto-fix succeeds and re-verification passes, skip the manager entirely for that iteration.

This is the "dynamic delegation" principle: don't send trivial work to expensive models.

Files to modify:
- `crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs`: Add try_auto_fix() between verifier and routing
- `coordination/src/verifier/report.rs`: Add helper to extract fixable errors with suggestions
title: Auto-fix trivial verifier failures without LLM delegation

description: The manager agent has a 45-minute timeout (AGENT_TIMEOUT in orchestrator.rs). close_reason: Implemented: token accuracy (char-based + safety margin), priority-scored context trimming, iteration delta memory, SourceFileProvider caching reader
description:
Fix token counting in coordination/src/work_packet/types.rs.

WorkPacket::estimated_tokens() uses json.len() / 4 where String::len() returns byte count not character count. Multi-byte UTF-8 characters cause overestimation.

FIX in estimated_tokens() method:

description:
UNANIMOUS CONSENSUS (4/4 models)

The current ContextPacker uses 'first 30 lines of each file' as context — this is spatial, not semantic. All 4 reviewing models flagged this as a critical weakness causing hallucinated APIs and wasted context budget.

Replace with AST-aware context extraction:
1. Integrate tree-sitter-rust for Rust AST parsing
2. Extract semantic units: full function bodies at error spans, struct/enum definitions, trait bounds, impl blocks
3. Score context by relevance: error spans > call sites > modified files > imports > headers
4. Include symbol-to-file map so the LLM knows 'where to edit' without searching

Specific improvements to build_file_contexts():
- Instead of first 30 lines, extract the function/impl containing the error span
- Include trait definitions referenced by trait bound errors
- Include callers/callees of modified functions
- Score each FileContext with a priority (Error > Modified > Dependency > Header)

Consider: headless rust-analyzer integration as a higher-fidelity alternative to tree-sitter. Start with tree-sitter for speed, upgrade to RA later.

In process_issue() main loop, after verifier runs (line ~456), compare error count to previous iteration. If errors increased by 50%+, revert changes and retry with strict constraints.

   c. Set a flag to add 'STRICT: change ONLY the lines reported in the error' to next iteration prompt
3. If circuit breaker fires twice consecutively, force escalation (set escalation tier to next level)

close_reason: Added try_claim() to IssueTracker with check-then-act semantics. BeadsBridge verifies status via bd show before claiming. Main loop iterates sorted candidates until one is claimed.
description:
The orchestrator has a check-then-act race: it calls list_open(), sorts by priority, picks the first issue, then calls update_status(in_progress). If two orchestrator instances run simultaneously (or two swarm loops), both will pick the same P1 issue, leading to duplicate work, git conflicts, and wasted inference credits.

Fix: Either implement an atomic claim_next_available() in BeadsBridge that combines list+claim in a single operation, or handle the case where update_status returns 'already claimed' and retry with the next issue. Consider adding a locking mechanism (file lock or beads-level CAS).

description:
STRONG CONSENSUS (3/4 models, builds on existing beefcake-swarm-481)

Use DSRs (dspy-rs) not as a per-agent prompt hack, but as a POLICY LAYER that optimizes how the swarm communicates with models.

Architecture:
1. DSRs sits BETWEEN ContextPacker and LLM call
2. Define typed Signatures for each agent role (Implementer, Validator, Planner, Adversary)
3. Define reward signals from deterministic gates:
   - +1.0 for all-green first try
   - +0.2 for green in <3 iterations with small patch
   - -1.0 for regression introduced
   - -0.5 for timeout/hang
   - -0.3 for validator risk flag
4. Treat prompt as: Template (stable) + Policy Parameters (learned)
   - Learned params: plan-vs-code ratio, tests-first toggle, max patch aggressiveness, context ordering
5. A/B test prompt variants using the existing ensemble infrastructure
6. Store prompt versions with success rate metrics in RocksDB

Offline optimization loop: replay past issues through DSRs optimizer to find better prompt configurations. Deploy optimized prompts, measure on new issues, iterate.

Depends on: agent traits extraction (beefcake-swarm-7ny), DSRs evaluation (beefcake-swarm-481)
Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/ (prompt policy module), coordination/src/ensemble/
title: Integrate DSRs as prompt policy optimization layer

close_reason: Added ChangeContract, Invariant, TestPlanEntry, ContractRiskLevel, TestPlanKind types to work_packet module. WorkPacket gets change_contract: Option<ChangeContract> field. All 7 construction sites updated. Backwards-compatible via serde(default). 5 new tests pass.
description:
UNANIMOUS CONSENSUS (4/4 models: G3-Pro, GPT-5.2-Codex, GPT-5.2, Opus 4.5)

The current loop goes straight to code generation without formalizing intent. Add a Planner agent (can use the fast 14B model) that runs BEFORE the Implementer and produces a structured 'change contract':

- Acceptance criteria (what must be true when done)
- Invariants / behavioral promises (what must NOT change)
- Files expected to change (scoping)
- Risk classification (API break risk, concurrency risk, security risk)
- Test plan: which tests to add/modify, what failure proves correctness

The contract becomes:
1. A constraint for the Implementer (bounded search space)
2. A checklist for the Validator (check against plan, not just 'does it look right')
3. An audit trail (what was intended vs what happened)
4. Partial rollback points (if step 3/5 fails, retry from step 3)

Implementation: Add a PlannerAgent trait and struct in swarm-agents, produce a ChangeContract type in coordination/. The contract feeds into WorkPacket.constraints and WorkPacket.decisions. The Validator receives the contract alongside the diff.

G3-Pro calls this 'Legislator-Executor'. GPT-5.2-Codex calls it '3-stage contract + selection'. Both identify it as the highest-impact architectural change.

Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/ (new planner module), coordination/src/ (new contract types), crates/swarm-agents/src/main.rs
title: Add Planner agent with structured change contracts

description:
STRONG CONSENSUS (3/4 models)

Agent-generated code can do ANYTHING — including filesystem deletion, network exfiltration, command execution in build.rs, or infinite loops in tests. On an HPC cluster with shared NFS, this is catastrophic.

Run all verification gates in a sandbox:
1. Use bubblewrap (bwrap) or firejail on Linux to sandbox cargo commands
2. Restrictions: no network access, restricted filesystem (only worktree + target dirs), CPU/memory/time caps
3. Per-test timeouts (not just per-gate — the existing gate_timeout_secs config isn't enforced)
4. Kill process trees on timeout (not just the parent process)
5. Detect 'test passed but took 10x longer than baseline' as suspicious

Also add: pre-check for dangerous patterns before running (new unsafe blocks, std::process::Command, std::fs::remove_dir_all, network calls).

Note: VerifierConfig already has gate_timeout_secs (line 28 in pipeline.rs) but it's not enforced — gates use blocking Command::output() with no timeout.

Files: coordination/src/verifier/pipeline.rs, coordination/src/verifier/ (new sandbox module)
title: Sandbox verification pipeline (treat agent code as untrusted)

close_reason: Implemented tokio::select! shutdown_signal() at all 3 process_issue call sites. SIGINT/SIGTERM triggers worktree cleanup + beads reset to open. Pushed: swarm/beefcake-swarm-mu1
description:
The orchestrator has no signal handler. If killed via SIGINT (Ctrl+C) or SIGTERM (deployment, systemd stop) while a worktree is active, the worktree remains on disk and registered in git, causing disk space leaks and 'already checked out' errors on restart.

Fix: Use tokio::signal::ctrl_c() and tokio::signal::unix::signal(SignalKind::terminate()) to catch shutdown signals. When received:
1. Set a shutdown flag (AtomicBool or tokio::sync::watch)
2. Check the flag at the top of each loop iteration
3. On shutdown: log the in-progress issue ID, call worktree_bridge.remove_worktree(), update beads status back to 'open', then exit cleanly

Consider wrapping the active worktree path in an Arc<Mutex<Option<String>>> so the signal handler knows what to clean up. Requires the remove_worktree() method from the worktree cleanup issue.

Depends on: worktree cleanup method (beefcake-swarm-rb2)

description:
ContextPacker::trim_to_budget() in coordination/src/context_packer/packer.rs (line 348) drops file_contexts from the end via pop(). The ordering guarantee is implicit and fragile.

FIX (2 parts):

PART 1 - Add priority field to FileContext in coordination/src/work_packet/types.rs:
Add a 'priority: u8' field to the FileContext struct (after 'relevance'). Use these values:
- 0 = Error context (highest, never trim first)
- 1 = Modified file content
- 2 = File header / structural context
- 3 = Reference context (lowest, trim first)
Default to 2 if not specified. Add #[serde(default = "default_priority")] with fn default_priority() -> u8 { 2 }.

PART 2 - Sort before trimming in trim_to_budget() (line 348-354):
Before the while loop, sort file_contexts by priority descending (highest priority number last) so pop() removes lowest-priority contexts first:
  packet.file_contexts.sort_by(|a, b| a.priority.cmp(&b.priority));

PART 3 - Update call sites in generator.rs and packer.rs:
Set priority field when constructing FileContext instances:
- In generator.rs build_error_contexts(): priority = 0
- In generator.rs build_file_contexts() for modified files: priority = 1  
- In generator.rs build_file_contexts() for headers: priority = 2
- In packer.rs pack_initial() for worktree files: priority = 2

FILES: coordination/src/work_packet/types.rs, coordination/src/context_packer/packer.rs, coordination/src/work_packet/generator.rs

ACCEPTANCE: cargo test -p coordination passes. Add a test that verifies trim_to_budget drops low-priority contexts before high-priority ones. cargo fmt and cargo clippy clean.
notes: SWARM-READY: Add one field to struct, one sort_by call, set priority values at ~4 construction sites. All in coordination crate.
title: Priority-scored context trimming in ContextPacker

close_reason: Converted 14 tracing macro sites in main.rs and worktree_bridge.rs from string interpolation to structured fields (error = %e, stderr = %stderr.trim(), branch = %branch). Log aggregators can now parse error types independently.
description:
Error logging uses string interpolation: error!('Failed to create worktree: {e}'). This embeds the error message in the format string, making it impossible for log aggregators (Loki, Datadog) to parse the error type separately from the message.

Fix: Use structured fields in tracing macros throughout main.rs and worktree_bridge.rs:
- error!(error = %e, issue_id = %id, 'Failed to create worktree') — Display format
- error!(error = ?e, issue_id = %id, 'Failed to create worktree') — Debug format (more detail)
- warn!(iteration, feedback = %result.feedback, 'Validator FAILED')

This allows querying logs by error type, issue_id, or iteration number independently.

description:
WorktreeBridge::create() in crates/swarm-agents/src/worktree_bridge.rs fails if branch swarm/<id> already exists from a previous failed run.

FIX in create() method (around line 74):
1. Before 'git worktree add -b swarm/<id>', check if branch exists: git branch --list swarm/<id>
2. If branch exists, delete it: git branch -D swarm/<id>
3. Also prune stale worktrees: git worktree prune
4. Then proceed with the normal git worktree add

Also check if worktree directory already exists at base_dir/<id> and remove it first (rm -rf or git worktree remove --force).

ACCEPTANCE: cargo test -p swarm-agents passes. Add a test that calls create() twice with the same ID (second call should succeed after cleanup). cargo fmt and cargo clippy clean.
title: WorktreeBridge::create should clean up stale branches before creating

close_reason: Implemented: fallback chain for detached HEAD (CI env vars → git name-rev → detached@sha), 3 tests
description:
WorkPacketGenerator::git_branch() in coordination/src/work_packet/generator.rs (line 394) uses 'git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD' which returns the literal string 'HEAD' when in detached HEAD state (common in CI, after checkout of a specific commit, or in freshly created worktrees before the first commit).

This means the WorkPacket.branch field becomes 'HEAD' which is misleading in the prompt sent to the LLM.

FIX in git_branch() method (line 394-402):
1. If result == 'HEAD', check CI env vars: CI_COMMIT_REF_NAME, GITHUB_HEAD_REF, BRANCH_NAME
2. If still 'HEAD', try 'git name-rev --name-only HEAD' and strip remotes/ prefix
3. If still 'HEAD', compute 'detached@<short-sha>' via 'git rev-parse --short HEAD'

The method signature stays fn git_branch(&self) -> Option<String>. Only the body changes.

FILES: coordination/src/work_packet/generator.rs (git_branch method, lines 394-402)

ACCEPTANCE: cargo test -p coordination passes. Add a unit test that mocks or simulates detached HEAD and verifies the fallback chain. cargo fmt --all -- --check and cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean.
notes: SWARM-READY: 5-10 line change in a single method. Fallback chain is well-specified. Fully additive logic.
title: Handle detached HEAD in WorkPacketGenerator

description:
UNANIMOUS CONSENSUS (4/4 models)

For hard/risky tasks, a single implementer often gets stuck in local minima. All 4 models propose spawning multiple implementations in parallel.

Design:
1. Classify issue difficulty (simple vs hard) based on: error category history, file count, risk classification from Planner
2. For hard tasks, spawn 2-3 Implementers in parallel with different strategies:
   - Different temperature settings (0.2 conservative, 0.8 creative)
   - Different system prompts ('minimal fix' vs 'refactor for clarity')
   - Different model tiers (14B fast attempt + 72B deep attempt)
3. Each produces a patch in its own worktree
4. Run Verifier on all patches in parallel (CPU-bound, manageable)
5. If multiple pass, Validator ranks and picks the best (most concise, least risk)
6. If none pass, combine insights from all attempts for the retry context

This trades compute for success rate. G3-Pro: 'Parallel Speculative Decoding'. GPT-5.2: 'Selection via competition — turn merges into a market'.

description:
Phase 3 Skill Library. Build a persistent skill library that indexes successful patterns from retrospectives. Skills are keyed by trigger context (error category + file pattern + task type) and store the approach that worked. When a new task matches a skill's trigger, the skill is injected into the work packet as a hint.

Depends on: issues 1 (telemetry), 2 (per-agent tracking), 7 (retrospective), 9 (knowledge capture)
Key files: NEW coordination/src/analytics/skills.rs, coordination/src/work_packet/types.rs, crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs
Crates: coordination + swarm-agents

Acceptance criteria:
- Skill struct: id, trigger (SkillTrigger), approach (String), success_count, failure_count, confidence
- SkillTrigger struct: error_categories (Vec), file_patterns (Vec<glob>), task_type (Option)
- SkillLibrary: add_skill(), find_matching(context: &TaskContext) -> Vec<Skill> (ranked by confidence)
- Confidence formula: success_count / (success_count + failure_count) with minimum sample threshold
- RocksDB persistence (reuse existing state infrastructure)
- WorkPacket gains skill_hints: Vec<SkillHint> field
- Orchestrator queries library before dispatching, injects matching skills
- Unit tests: skill matching, confidence scoring, persistence round-trip
title: Implement skill library with pattern indexing

description:
Despite compact prompts (~400 chars user message) and read_file truncation, ~33% of Worker tier iterations still produce text analysis instead of tool calls. This is stochastic and prompt-content-dependent — the same prompt format sometimes triggers tool calls and sometimes doesn't.

Investigation areas:
1. **Temperature tuning**: test temp=0.05-0.1 to escape deterministic bad paths while maintaining reliability (current: 0.0)
2. **System prompt optimization**: the general_coder preamble (~1300 chars) may have content patterns that suppress tool calls. Try a minimal preamble.
3. **Retry on text-only response**: if the agent returns text without tool calls on turn 1, automatically retry the same prompt (stochastic behavior means retries may work)
4. **Qwen3.5-397B comparison**: the larger model may handle tool calling better — test when Q4_K_M download completes

Current reliability: 67% (4/6 iterations make tool calls). Target: >90%.

close_reason: JsonSchema derives added to all specialist response types in contracts.rs, pipeline.rs, reviewer.rs. Enables Rig structured extraction via agent.extract().
description:
Use Rig's structured extraction (agent.extract()) with schemars::JsonSchema derive to force LLMs to return typed Rust structs instead of parsing free-text responses.

Background: Currently the swarm parses LLM text output manually or uses basic JSON parsing. Rig's extract() method generates a JSON schema from Rust types and constrains the LLM to return conforming output, eliminating parsing failures.

Pattern from rig-examples:
```rust
#[derive(Deserialize, schemars::JsonSchema)]
struct CodeReview {
    severity: String,
    issues: Vec<Issue>,
    suggestion: String,
    confidence: f64,
}

let review: CodeReview = agent.extract("Review this code: ...").await?;
```

Target types for structured extraction:
1. VerifierResult — pass/fail, error categories, fix suggestions
2. WorkPacketAnalysis — task classification, file contexts, key symbols, complexity score
3. CodeReviewOutput — severity, issues list, suggestions, confidence
4. EscalationDecision — should_escalate, reason, recommended_tier, context summary
5. PlannerOutput — steps list, file targets, dependency graph, risk assessment

Implementation:
1. Add schemars dependency to swarm-agents Cargo.toml
2. Define JsonSchema-deriving structs for each target type
3. Replace agent.prompt() with agent.extract() where structured output is needed
4. Add fallback parsing for when extraction fails (model returns non-conforming JSON)
5. Test with both Qwen3.5 (local) and cloud models to verify schema compliance

Acceptance: All 5 target types use structured extraction with >95% parse success rate across model tiers.
title: Structured extraction via schemars::JsonSchema for typed LLM outputs

Either: (1) allow pipe specifically for commands in the allowlist (rg, grep, find), (2) add a list_files_matching or search_files tool that combines grep+head internally, or (3) run commands through a shell wrapper that sanitizes dangerous metacharacters while allowing pipes. Current behavior causes models to waste 2+ turns on failed search attempts.

description: Study how production coding agent harnesses handle: (1) tool call formatting/serialization to LLM backends, (2) agent loop retry/error handling (our Rig loop deadlocks on 500 errors), (3) file editing strategies (line-level vs content replacement), (4) verification/quality gates, (5) context packing and prompt construction. Target harnesses: claude-code (TypeScript), gemini-cli, codex (OpenAI), Pi, opencode, oh-my-opencode. Extract patterns we can adopt in our Rig-based system without full rewrites. Triggered by: llama.cpp autoparser parse failures blocking all inference, and futex deadlock in agent loop on HTTP errors.
notes: Concrete bugs found during Epic A dogfood that production harnesses handle: (1) HTTP client hangs indefinitely (futex_do_wait) when inference server is restarted mid-request — no timeout, no reconnect, no error propagation. Claude Code and Codex use per-request timeouts with exponential backoff. (2) Rig agent loop has no circuit breaker on 500 errors — silently blocks forever instead of failing fast. (3) No heartbeat/liveness check during LLM generation — worker can't detect server crash mid-stream. (4) dogfood-loop.sh SIGTERM doesn't propagate to child swarm-agents processes — they become orphans.
title: Research coding harness patterns from Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex, opencode, etc.

description: Claude Code team insight: don't dump all context upfront, let the agent discover it layer by layer. Current packed context front-loads everything. Adopt a 'read on demand' pattern: provide file list + summary, let agent request details. Could reduce prompt tokens by 50-70%.
title: FW: Progressive disclosure — lazy context loading

description: iPad users won't have a keyboard attached for most lab monitoring tasks. Audit all critical experiment control flows for text input requirements: (1) replace numeric text inputs with sliders where appropriate (start/stop values for scans), (2) device parameter changes via +/- stepper buttons instead of type-and-enter, (3) run naming via picker from recent runs list + optional suffix. NOT required: full settings editing, script editing, advanced configuration (these remain text-input on desktop). Goal: complete a standard scan start/monitor/stop workflow on iPad without the keyboard appearing.
title: T2.2: Keyboard-free workflow — critical controls accessible without typing

close_reason: Implemented in PR #49 and #50. bdh :init wired into worktree_bridge.rs with auth env vars.
description: When the orchestrator creates a worktree for an issue, it should run 'bdh :init' in that worktree with a unique alias (e.g., worker-<issue-id>) and worker role. This enables file reservations and agent messaging during subtask dispatch. Requires: BeadHub server running (BEADHUB_URL env var), bdh installed on ai-proxy. The .beadhub config file must be created before any agent tools run.
title: Wire bdh worktree init into orchestrator for multi-agent coordination

description:
Deliver iPad lab access by polishing the existing WASM egui build and validating grpc-web against real hardware. Outcome of the Slint evaluation (2026-03, eval/slint-gui-comparison): CONDITIONAL-GO for Slint but the iPad/monitoring goal is better served by the existing WASM stack at ~10x less effort (1-2 weeks vs 24 weeks). Full Slint migration indefinitely deferred.

Strategy: validate grpc-web runtime → polish WASM for touch/Safari → optimize camera frame display → conditional native wrapper for background alerts only if needed.

Decision basis: Two independent Gemini analyses + own E1-E5 evaluation agreed unanimously on Option C (WASM egui) over Option A (full Slint migration) or Option B (separate Slint iOS module). Slint evaluation docs archived on eval/slint-gui-comparison branch.
title: iPad/tablet remote monitoring — WASM egui deployment

description: Modify ReadFileTool in fs_tools.rs to emit span metadata with each read: start_line, end_line, content_sha256 for the returned window. Workers must cite this anchor when editing. This prevents stale-read edits where the file changed between read and edit. Pattern from OpenCode's freshness guards.
title: Add content hash anchors to read_file tool output

description:
File: crates/swarm-agents/src/notebook_bridge.rs

Change: Track the last query timestamp. If less than 1 second has elapsed since the last query, sleep the difference before proceeding. This prevents hitting Google API rate limits during rapid-fire queries.

Use std::time::Instant for tracking. Store it in the NotebookBridge struct (or as a thread-local/static if the struct is not mutable).

Acceptance: cargo build -p swarm-agents compiles. Two queries within 500ms result in the second being delayed.

Constraints: Do NOT modify any other file. Default rate limit is 1 query per second — no env var needed for this.
title: E4: Add basic rate limiting to NotebookBridge::query()

Orchestrator should: (1) check should_refresh() after each session, run analyze_and_refresh() when due, log RefreshReport; (2) optionally generate dashboard metrics on --dashboard flag or at session end. Files: crates/swarm-agents/src/orchestrator.rs, main.rs. Acceptance: KB refresh runs every N sessions, dashboard metrics available on demand.
title: Wire dashboard and KB refresh into orchestrator session lifecycle
